# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct, definition-based questions that test the recall level of **Bloom's L1 (Remember)**. Each carries **3 marks** and requires a precise, 4–6 sentence answer.

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define a Semantic Network. List any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Semantic Network** is a graph-based knowledge representation technique where **nodes** represent concepts (objects, events) and **labeled directed edges** represent semantic relations between them. It was proposed by **Ross Quillian in 1968** as a model of human associative memory.

**Limitations (any two):**
1. **Lack of standard semantics** — different systems used link labels inconsistently (e.g., IS-A vs. AKO vs. A-Kind-Of), so two networks cannot easily be merged.
2. **No exception handling** — properties inherited from parent classes cannot be cleanly overridden (the Penguin problem). Defaults are ad-hoc.
3. **No quantifier support** — it cannot precisely express "All birds fly" vs. "Some birds fly."
4. **Combinatorial explosion** — for n concepts, link count can be O(n²), making the network hard to maintain.
5. **No truth value tracking** — links are assumed true by default; there is no mechanism for default vs. asserted truth.

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is an Ontology? Mention Gruber's 5 design principles for building ontologies.**

**Model Answer:**
An **Ontology** is a formal, explicit specification of a shared conceptualization of a domain (Gruber, 1993). It defines the **classes, properties, individuals, and axioms** used to model that domain, enabling both humans and machines to share a common understanding.

**Gruber's 5 design principles:**
1. **Clarity** — definitions should be objective, complete, and documented.
2. **Coherence** — the ontology's inferences should not contradict its axioms.
3. **Extendibility** — new terms can be added without breaking existing definitions.
4. **Minimal Encoding Bias** — should be independent of any specific symbol-level encoding.
5. **Minimal Ontological Commitment** — should make the weakest claims, sufficient for the task.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE 2024 Scheme format: One full question of 14 marks with **sub-part (a) for 7 marks** and **sub-part (b) for 7 marks**. There is an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A *or* Question B. Both are provided below.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**A. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **Knowledge-Based System (KBS)**. List and briefly explain its major components. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

A **Knowledge-Based System (KBS)** is an AI system that emulates the decision-making ability of a human expert by storing domain knowledge explicitly and reasoning over it. The standard architecture consists of the following components (refer Section 4.2 diagram):

1. **Knowledge Base (KB):** The declarative store of facts, rules, and axioms about the domain. Example: `IF (fever AND cough) THEN suspect(influenza)`. The KB is built via a knowledge engineer interviewing a human expert.

2. **Inference Engine (IE):** The reasoning module. It applies forward chaining (data-driven, used in monitoring/diagnosis) or backward chaining (goal-driven, used in design/planning) to derive new facts. It uses algorithms like Rete for efficient pattern matching.

3. **Working Memory:** The temporary storage for the current problem's input facts and intermediate conclusions.

4. **Explanation Facility:** Generates human-readable traces of *why* a conclusion was reached, by recording the rule-firing sequence. This is critical for user trust in medical/legal domains.

5. **Knowledge Acquisition Module:** The front-end that allows experts to insert, modify, or delete knowledge in the KB.

6. **User Interface:** The GUI or natural-language interface used by the end-user to query the system.

**Diagrammatic representation:** As shown in Section 4.2 — the arrows indicate data/control flow: User → Interface → IE → KB / WM, with bidirectional feedback.

**Example of a KBS:** **MYCIN** (1976) — a medical expert system for diagnosing bacterial infections and recommending antibiotic therapy. It used ~600 rules and backward chaining.

**A. (b)** Explain **Semantic Networks** in detail. Compare **IS-A** and **INSTANCE-OF** links with suitable examples. Demonstrate property inheritance with a worked example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

A **Semantic Network** is a graph-based KR formalism. Nodes = concepts, edges = labeled semantic relations. The two most critical link types are:

**IS-A Link (Hypernym / Subclass):**
- Connects a *subclass* to a *superclass*.
- Denotes a "kind-of" relationship.
- Example: `Sparrow IS-A Bird` — A sparrow is a **kind of** bird.
- Enables property inheritance: any property of `Bird` propagates to `Sparrow`.

**INSTANCE-OF Link (Membership):**
- Connects an *individual* to a *class*.
- Denotes a "membership" relationship.
- Example: `Tweety INSTANCE-OF Sparrow` — Tweety is a **member of** the sparrow class.
- Does *not* define a new sub-class.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | IS-A | INSTANCE-OF |
|---------|------|-------------|
| Connects | Subclass → Superclass | Individual → Class |
| Nature | Class-to-class | Object-to-class |
| Inheritance | Yes (properties propagate to all instances) | No (individual inherits, but does not create sub-class) |
| Example | `Penguin IS-A Bird` | `Pingu INSTANCE-OF Penguin` |

**Worked Inheritance Example:**

> Given: `Rex INSTANCE-OF Dog`, `Dog IS-A Mammal`, `Mammal HAS-FUR yes`
> Infer: `HAS-FUR(Rex)` = ?

**Solution:**
Step 1: `Rex` is an instance of `Dog`.
Step 2: `Dog` is a subclass of `Mammal`.
Step 3: By transitivity of IS-A, `Rex` is *implicitly* a member of `Mammal`.
Step 4: `Mammal` has property `HAS-FUR = yes`.
Step 5: By inheritance rule, `Rex` inherits `HAS-FUR = yes`.
**∴ `Rex` has fur.**

**Exception handling demonstration (Penguin problem):**
If we add `Penguin IS-A Bird` and `Bird CAN-FLY yes`, then a naive semantic network would say `Penguin CAN-FLY yes` — wrong. The solution: add an explicit `CAN-FLY: no` exception slot on `Penguin` that **overrides** the parent. The reasoner first checks the most-specific class; if an exception is present, it wins.

**Real-world applications of semantic networks:**
- **WordNet** (lexical database of English) — organizes ~117,000 synsets via IS-A.
- **ConceptNet** — common-sense knowledge base used in chatbots.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 – Alternative]`

**B. (a)** Define an **Ontology**. Explain its major components. With a suitable example, show how an ontology is represented using **RDF triples** and **OWL class hierarchies**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Definition (Gruber):** An ontology is an *explicit, formal specification of a shared conceptualization* of a domain.

**Major Components (refer Section 2.3.1):**
1. **Individuals (Instances)** — concrete entities (e.g., `Kerala`, `India`).
2. **Classes (Concepts)** — sets of individuals (e.g., `State`, `Country`).
3. **Object Properties** — binary relations between individuals (e.g., `hasCapital`).
4. **Data Properties** — relations from individuals to literal values (e.g., `population`).
5. **Axioms** — logical constraints (e.g., `State ⊑ ∃hasCapital.City` — every state has a capital city).
6. **T-Box / A-Box split** — T-Box holds class/property definitions, A-Box holds individual assertions.

**Example — Indian Geography Ontology:**

**Step 1: Define the class hierarchy (T-Box).**
- `Country ⊑ Thing`
- `State ⊑ Thing`
- `City ⊑ Thing`
- `State ⊑ ∃partOf.Country` (axiom: every state is part of a country)
- `Country ⊑ ∃hasCapital.City` (axiom: every country has a capital city)

**Step 2: Define the object properties.**
- `partOf ⊑ TopObjectProperty`
- `hasCapital ⊑ TopObjectProperty`
- `hasCapital ⊑ ∃1.City` (functional — a country has exactly one capital)

**Step 3: Add individuals (A-Box).**
- `India INSTANCE-OF Country`
- `Kerala INSTANCE-OF State`
- `Thiruvananthapuram INSTANCE-OF City`
- `Kerala partOf India`
- `India hasCapital NewDelhi`

**Step 4: RDF Triple Representation.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\text{India},\ \texttt{rdf:type},\ \text{Country}) \\
&(\text{Kerala},\ \texttt{rdf:type},\ \text{State}) \\
&(\text{Thiruvananthapuram},\ \texttt{rdf:type},\ \text{City}) \\
&(\text{Kerala},\ \text{partOf},\ \text{India}) \\
&(\text{India},\ \text{hasCapital},\ \text{NewDelhi}) \\
&(\text{State},\ \texttt{rdfs:subClassOf},\ \text{Thing}) \\
&(\text{Country},\ \texttt{rdfs:subClassOf},\ \text{Thing})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: OWL Functional Syntax.**
```
Declaration(Class(:Country))
Declaration(Class(:State))
Declaration(ObjectProperty(:partOf))
SubClassOf(:State ObjectSomeValuesFrom(:partOf :Country))
SubClassOf(:Country ObjectSomeValuesFrom(:hasCapital :City))
```

**B. (b)** Explain the **Semantic Web Layer Cake**. Discuss the roles of **RDF, RDFS, and OWL** with one example each. Mention any two ontology engineering methodologies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

The **Semantic Web Layer Cake** (a W3C reference model) is a stack of technologies, where each layer uses the capabilities of the layers below. (Refer Section 4.3 for the visual diagram.)

**Layer-by-layer roles:**

1. **URI + Unicode (Foundation):** Unique identification of resources and global text encoding.
2. **XML + XML Schema:** Provides structured document syntax and datatype definitions.
3. **RDF (Resource Description Framework):** The *data layer*. Represents any fact as a triple `(Subject, Predicate, Object)`. Example:
   `(Rex, rdf:type, Dog)` — Rex is a Dog.
4. **RDFS (RDF Schema):** Adds *vocabulary* for describing classes and properties.
   - `rdfs:Class`, `rdfs:subClassOf`, `rdfs:domain`, `rdfs:range`.
   - Example: `Dog rdfs:subClassOf Mammal` — Dog is a sub-class of Mammal.
5. **OWL (Web Ontology Language):** Adds *axioms and restrictions* that RDFS cannot express.
   - Supports cardinality, equivalence, disjointness, transitive/functional/symmetric properties.
   - Example: `hasCapital rdf:type owl:FunctionalProperty` — a country has *at most one* capital.
6. **SPARQL:** Query language for RDF, analogous to SQL for relational databases.
   - Example: `SELECT ?c WHERE { ?c rdf:type :State . ?c :partOf :India }`
7. **SWRL (Semantic Web Rule Language):** Adds Horn-clause rules to OWL.
8. **SKOS:** Lexical layer for thesauri and knowledge organization systems.
9. **Top layer — Logic, Proof, Trust:** Used for reasoning, validation, and provenance.

**Two Ontology Engineering Methodologies:**

**1. Uschold & King (1995) — METHONTOLOGY-inspired:**
- Steps: **Purpose → Domain Analysis → Conceptualization → Formalization → Implementation → Evaluation → Documentation → Maintenance.**
- Strength: Mature, widely taught.
- Weakness: Iterative feedback between steps is manual.

**2. Gruninger & Fox (1995) — TOVE (Toronto Virtual Enterprise):**
- Steps: 
  1. Identify motivating scenarios.
  2. Formulate informal competency questions (CQs).
  3. Specify terminology formally in first-order logic.
  4. Formalize competency questions as queries.
  5. Define axioms and constraints.
  6. Ensure the ontology answers all CQs.
- Strength: Logic-driven, rigorous, uses competency questions as test cases.
- Weakness: Requires expertise in first-order logic.

**Other notable methods (for extra credit in viva):**
- **METHONTOLOGY** (Fernández, 1997) — Spanish origin, used in chemical ontologies.
- **On-To-Knowledge** (Staab & Sure, 2002) — for enterprise knowledge management.
- **NeOn** (Suárez-Figueroa, 2010) — networked, collaborative ontology engineering.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where You Will Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing `Rex IS-A Dog` instead of `Rex INSTANCE-OF Dog`** — direct 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing T-Box with A-Box** — examiners ask this directly. T-Box = schema; A-Box = data.
> 3. **Not showing inheritance exception** for the Penguin problem in semantic network answers — 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Forgetting to define Gruber's 5 principles** when asked about ontology design — they are tested almost every semester.
> 5. **Writing the Semantic Web Layer Cake in the wrong order** — memorize bottom-up: URI, XML, RDF, RDFS, OWL, SPARQL, SWRL.
> 6. **Omitting prefix declarations** in RDF examples (e.g., `@prefix rdf:`) — KTU deducts marks for syntactically incorrect RDF.
> 7. **Not drawing the network diagram** in semantic network questions — minimum 2 marks reserved for the figure.

---

## 5.3 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print & Stick on Your Wall — The 30-Second Revision Card**

### ✅ Knowledge-Based Systems (KBS)
- KBS = **Knowledge Base + Inference Engine + Working Memory + Explanation Facility + UI + Acquisition Module**.
- Memory trick: **K.I.W.E.U.E.** (Knowledge, Inference, Working memory, Explanation, User, Editor).
- Classic examples: **MYCIN** (medicine), **DENDRAL** (chemistry), **XCON** (computer config).

### ✅ Semantic Networks
- Introduced by **Quillian (1968)**.
- **Nodes** = concepts, **Edges** = labeled semantic relations.
- **IS-A** = subclass-to-class (Sparrow IS-A Bird).
- **INSTANCE-OF** = individual-to-class (Tweety INSTANCE-OF Sparrow).
- **HAS-A / PART-OF** = aggregation / meronymy.
- Inference uses **transitivity of IS-A** + **inheritance**.
- **Exception handling** needed for the *Penguin problem*.

### ✅ Frames (Brief — often tested alongside)
- Introduced by **Minsky (1975)**.
- Frame = object with **slots** and **values**.
- Slots can have `default`, `if-needed`, `if-added` demons for procedural attachment.
- Frame systems organize multiple frames into hierarchies with IS-A inheritance.

### ✅ Ontologies — Core Facts
- **Definition:** Gruber (1993) — *"explicit, formal specification of a shared conceptualization."*
- **Components:** Individuals, Classes, Object Properties, Data Properties, Axioms, Rules.
- **T-Box** = terminology (schema); **A-Box** = assertions (data).
- **5 design principles** (Gruber): Clarity, Coherence, Extendibility, Minimal Encoding Bias, Minimal Ontological Commitment.
- **4 types by generality:** Top-level, Domain, Task, Application.

### ✅ Semantic Web Stack (Layer Cake)
- Order (bottom-up): **URI → Unicode → XML → XML Schema → RDF → RDFS → OWL → SPARQL → SWRL/SKOS → Logic/Proof/Trust**.
- **RDF** = triples `(S, P, O)`.
- **RDFS** = adds `subClassOf`, `domain`, `range`.
- **OWL** = adds cardinality, equivalence, restrictions, property characteristics.
- **SPARQL** = query language for RDF.

### ✅ Ontology Engineering Methodologies
- **Uschold & King** (1995) — 7-step process.
- **Gruninger & Fox TOVE** (1995) — competency-question-driven.
- **METHONTOLOGY** (1997) — Spanish project.
- **NeOn** (2010) — networked, collaborative.

### ✅ Tools You May Reference
- **Protégé** — Stanford's open-source ontology editor.
- **Jena / RDF4J** — Java APIs for RDF.
- **HermiT / Pellet** — OWL reasoners.
- **TopBraid Composer** — commercial ontology IDE.
- **DBpedia** — large public ontology derived from Wikipedia.

### ✅ Quick Symbol Reference
- $A \sqsubseteq B$ → A subclass of B
- $A \equiv B$ → A and B equivalent
- $A \sqcap B$ → intersection
- $\exists R.C$ → existential restriction
- $\forall R.C$ → universal restriction
- $C(a)$ → a is an instance of C
- T-Box = $\mathcal{T}$ ; A-Box = $\mathcal{A}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** When answering any KBS / Semantic Network / Ontology question in KTU 2024 Scheme, always (1) **define** the concept, (2) **draw the diagram**, (3) **give a worked example**, and (4) **state applications**. A 4-layer answer consistently scores 13–14/14.

---
*End of Module 2 Notes — Knowledge-Based Approaches, Semantic Networks and Ontologies*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semantic Networks: Structure, Properties, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAIT521 - KNOWLEDGE ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Semantic Networks: Structure, Properties, and Applications # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Semantic Network** (also called a *semantic net* or *associative network*) is a directed graph-based knowledge representation formalism used in Knowledge Engineering to encode **concepts**, **entities**, and the **semantic relationships** between them. Formally, a semantic network is defined as a labeled, directed graph $G = (V, E, L)$, where: $$\begin{aligned} G &= (V, E, L) \\ V &= \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\} \quad \text{(set of nodes / concepts / objects)} \\ E &= \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_m\} \quad \text{(set of directed edges / relations)} \\ L &: E \rightarrow \Sigma \quad \text{(labeling function mapping edges to relation names)} \end{aligned}$$ - **Nodes ($V$)** represent *physical objects*, *abstract concepts*, *events*, or *situations*. - **Edges ($E$)** represent *semantic relations* (associations) between nodes and are typically labeled with relationship names such as `IS-A`, `A-KIND-OF`, `HAS-PART`, `INSTANCE-OF`, `AGENT`, `INSTRUMENT`, `LOCATION`, `TIME`, etc. - **Directionality** of edges encodes the semantic asymmetry: the relation is read from **tail (source)** to **head (target)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Semantic networks are one of the foundational *graph-based* knowledge representation techniques studied under Module 2. They were originally proposed by **M. Ross Quillian (1968)** as a model of human semantic memory and later popularized by researchers like **William A. Woods** and **Ronald J. Brachman** in AI. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are drawing a **family tree on a whiteboard**. You write names inside ovals (nodes) and connect them with lines labeled "father-of", "mother-of", "married-to", etc. Now, instead of just family, imagine a much bigger web where you connect: - `Birds` → `IS-A` → `Animals` - `Sparrow` → `INSTANCE-OF` → `Bird` - `Sparrow` → `CAN` → `Fly` - `Sparrow` → `HAS-PART` → `Wings` - `Wings` → `USED-FOR` → `Flying` This whole web — the *ovals* (nodes) + the *labeled arrows* (relations) — **is a semantic network.** It is essentially the *graph-theoretic encoding* of how a human mind associates meanings, which is why it is called a "semantic" network (from the Greek *semantikos* = "significant, meaningful"). > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** A semantic network is just a *graph whose edges carry meaning*. Unlike a plain graph in data structures (where edges are unlabeled), every edge in a semantic network tells you **how** two concepts are related. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Structural Properties - **Node degree** of a concept $v_i$: the number of edges incident to $v_i$, denoted $\deg(v_i)$. - **Semantic Distance** between two nodes $v_i$ and $v_j$: the length of the **shortest path** between them, often denoted $d(v_i, v_j)$ and measured in **edges (hops)**. - **Connectivity**: a semantic network is **strongly connected** if every node is reachable from every other node via directed edges; otherwise it is partitioned into *strongly connected components (SCCs)*. - **Inheritance depth**: the longest chain of `IS-A` / `A-KIND-OF` edges from a leaf node to the root concept, typically ranging from **3 to 7 levels** in well-designed ontologies. - **Branching factor**: average out-degree of a node, usually between **2 and 6** in cognitive-plausible semantic nets. > [!TIP] > **Cognitive Fact:** The classic cognitive science benchmark *Collins & Quillian (1969)* showed that human reaction time for verifying statements like *"A canary can fly"* increases linearly with the **semantic distance** between `canary` and `fly` in the network. This is one of the original empirical validations of the semantic network model. ## 1.4 Historical Classification (Three Generations) | Generation | Era | Key Feature | Example System | |---|---|---|---| | **1st Gen** | 1960s–1970s | Simple labeled graphs; no inference engine | Quillian's TLC, Minsky's Frames (precursor) | | **2nd Gen** | 1970s–1980s | Partitioned networks, type-token distinction, attached procedures | NETL, KL-ONE | | **3rd Gen** | 1990s–Present | Description Logics, OWL-based formal ontologies | WordNet, ConceptNet, DBpedia, SNOMED-CT | --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Components of a Semantic Network A semantic network is built from **three primary atomic units** that combine to form a complete representational system: 1. **Conceptual Nodes (Tokens)** — The *nouns* of the network. They represent individual objects, classes, events, or states. Each node has a unique identifier and may carry an attached **data structure** (a "case frame" or "slot list"). 2. **Relation Labels (Link Types)** — The *verbs* of the network. They are drawn from a controlled vocabulary of semantic relations. 3. **Inheritance Mechanism** — A reasoning rule that allows properties of a *super-class* to be automatically inherited by its *sub-classes* and *instances* without explicit duplication. ## 2.2 Taxonomy of Semantic Relations Semantic relations fall into **four major families**, each carrying a different inferential load: ### (a) Taxonomic / Hierarchical Relations - `IS-A` — "X is a Y" (subset / specialization). - `A-KIND-OF` (AKO) — same as `IS-A` but class-to-class. - `INSTANCE-OF` — object-to-class link. ### (b) Meronymic / Part-Whole Relations - `PART-OF`, `HAS-PART`, `MEMBER-OF`, `STUFF-OF` — encode structural decomposition. ### (c) Case-Role / Thematic Relations - `AGENT`, `PATIENT`, `INSTRUMENT`, `LOCATION`, `TIME`, `SOURCE`, `GOAL`, `THEME` — derived from **Charles Fillmore's Case Grammar** (1968). ### (d) Attribute / Property Relations - `HAS-COLOR`, `HAS-SIZE`, `CAN`, `HAS-AGE` — describe properties or capabilities of nodes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "list the types of semantic relations", always present the **four-family classification above**. Examiners reward structured answers with named categories rather than ad-hoc lists. ## 2.3 Core Inference Operations The **three foundational inference operations** in any semantic network are: | # | Operation | Definition | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Inheritance** | A subclass $C_2$ inherits all properties of superclass $C_1$ if $C_2 \xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}} C_1$. | Deduction: "Sparrow can fly" → "All birds can fly" | | 2 | **Intersection Search** | Finding the common ancestors of two nodes along their `IS-A` paths. | Determining if two classes share a supertype. | | 3 | **Property Matching** | Comparing slot values of two nodes for equality, type, or value restriction. | Ontology alignment, instance classification. | ## 2.4 Partitioned Semantic Networks (Woods, 1975) William Woods introduced the critical **partitioned semantic network** to overcome the ambiguity of the original Quillian nets. In a partitioned network: - A single complex assertion is encoded as a **single partition** (a boxed sub-network). - The partition is connected to its constituent concept nodes by a special edge labeled `θ` (theta). - Variables inside the partition are bound, eliminating the *link ambiguity* problem (where a single relation could mean different things in different contexts). > [!NOTE] > The θ-notation (theta roles) in partitioned networks is the direct conceptual ancestor of **first-order logic variables** in Description Logics and modern OWL. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes the **symbolic notation, semantic relations, and inference rules** that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce from memory. There are no physical constants here, but **memorize the standard notation** — it appears in past university exam papers. | Symbol / Notation | Meaning | KTU-Standard Example | |---|---|---| | $G = (V, E, L)$ | Semantic net as a labeled directed graph | $V$ = concepts, $E$ = relations, $L$ = label set | | $v_i \xrightarrow{r} v_j$ | Directed labeled edge from $v_i$ to $v_j$ with relation $r$ | `Sparrow` $\xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}}$ `Bird` | | $\text{IS-A}$ | Subclass / specialization link | Dog $\xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}}$ Mammal | | $\text{AKO}$ | A-Kind-Of (class-to-class) | Carnivore $\xrightarrow{\text{AKO}}$ Mammal | | $\text{INSTANCE-OF}$ | Object-to-class link | Fido $\xrightarrow{\text{INSTANCE-OF}}$ Dog | | $\text{HAS-PART}$ | Meronymic part-whole link | Car $\xrightarrow{\text{HAS-PART}}$ Engine | | $\text{AGENT}, \text{PATIENT}$ | Thematic (case) roles | John $\xrightarrow{\text{AGENT}}$ hit $\xleftarrow{\text{PATIENT}}$ Ball | | $d(v_i, v_j)$ | Semantic distance (shortest path) | $d$(sparrow, animal) = 2 | | $\deg(v_i)$ | Degree of node $v_i$ | High-degree nodes = core concepts | | $\text{INHERIT}(P, C_1, C_2)$ | Property $P$ on $C_1$ inherited by $C_2$ via IS-A | INHERIT(can-fly, bird, sparrow) = TRUE | | $\theta$-partition | Variable binding in Woods' partitioned net | Bound variable in scoped sub-network | ## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility Semantic networks are not merely academic constructs. They are deployed at industrial scale in: - **Healthcare** — SNOMED-CT encodes $\sim$**350,000 medical concepts** with multi-million semantic relations for clinical decision support. - **Search Engines** — Google's *Knowledge Graph* (2012–present) uses a semantic graph of over **500 billion facts** about **5 billion entities** to power entity-aware search. - **Natural Language Processing** — **WordNet** (Princeton, 1995–) contains $\sim$**117,000 synsets** linked by hypernymy, hyponymy, meronymy, and antonymy relations. - **Cybersecurity** — **STIX** (Structured Threat Information eXpression) uses a semantic graph ontology to describe cyber threats and Indicators of Compromise (IoCs). - **Recommender Systems** — Pinterest's *PinSage* graph neural network treats user-item interactions as a semantic network. - **Linguistics & Cognitive Science** — **ConceptNet** (MIT, 2016) has $\sim$**34 million edges** over $\sim$**3 million concepts** in 78 languages. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Inference Procedures & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Major Network Formalisms The following decision matrix is a high-yield study artifact for KTU answer-writing. It directly compares the **three canonical graph-based knowledge representation formalisms** you must know for Module 2. | Property | Semantic Network | Frames | Description Logics (DL) | |---|---|---|---| | **Representation** | Labeled directed graph | Slot-filler structures | Formal logic (subset of FOL) | | **Primary Unit** | Node + labeled edge | Frame (object) with slots | Concept, Role, Individual | | **Inheritance** | Default (polymorphic) | Default (multiple) | Subsumption-based (decidable) | | **Expressiveness** | Medium | Medium | Tunable (EL, ALC, SROIQ) | | **Decidability** | Not guaranteed | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed (for specific DLs) | | **Inference Engine** | Marker passing, path search | Slot value propagation | Tableau algorithm | | **Example System** | WordNet, ConceptNet | FrameNet | OWL 2 (Protégé editor) | | **Creator** | Quillian (1968) | Minsky (1975) | Brachman & Levesque (1984) | ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Inference Procedure: Default Inheritance with Exceptions Consider a typical KTU question of the form: *"Given the following semantic net, infer whether 'Tweety can fly'."* The valuation scheme expects a **stepwise proof**. ### Problem Setup (canonical example) **Network assertions:** 1. `Tweety` $\xrightarrow{\text{INSTANCE-OF}}$ `Canary` 2. `Canary` $\xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}}$ `Bird` 3. `Bird` $\xrightarrow{\text{CAN}}$ `fly` 4. `Ostrich` $\xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}}$ `Bird` 5. `Ostrich` $\xrightarrow{\text{CAN-NOT}}$ `fly` 6. `Tweety` $\xrightarrow{\text{HAS-COLOR}}$ `yellow` ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the query and the target property.** We need to determine the truth value of the proposition: *Tweety can fly*. The target property is `CAN fly` and the subject is `Tweety`. **Step 2 — Check for an explicit local assertion.** Search the node `Tweety` for any direct edge labeled `CAN` or `CAN-NOT`. Result: *None present* (only HAS-COLOR yellow). So we must **inherit** upward. **Step 3 — Apply the inheritance algorithm along the IS-A path.** Walk from `Tweety` toward the root following `INSTANCE-OF` and `IS-A` edges: $$\text{Tweety} \xrightarrow{\text{INSTANCE-OF}} \text{Canary} \xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}} \text{Bird}$$ **Step 4 — Look up the property on each ancestor in order (closest first).** - On `Canary`: No `CAN fly` edge. - On `Bird`: `Bird` $\xrightarrow{\text{CAN}}$ `fly` ✓. **Step 5 — Check for blocking / overriding exceptions.** Search all nodes in the path and *all sibling sub-classes* (called **closed-world exception check**): - `Ostrich` (a sibling of Canary under Bird) has `CAN-NOT fly`. But `Tweety` is **not an instance of Ostrich** — the exception is **non-inheritable** because it is bound to `Ostrich` and its descendants, not to `Bird` as a whole. **Step 6 — Conclude via monotonic inheritance.** $$\text{INHERIT}(\text{CAN fly}, \text{Tweety}, \text{Bird}) = \text{TRUE}$$ **Answer:** *Yes, Tweety can fly.* The chain is `Tweety → Canary → Bird` and `Bird` has `CAN fly`. ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Computing Semantic Distance The **semantic distance** between two concepts in a network is the length of the **shortest path** along any directed relation. This is the metric KTU often asks students to compute. **Given the following mini-network:** $$\text{Mammal} \xleftarrow{\text{IS-A}} \text{Dog} \xleftarrow{\text{IS-A}} \text{Poodle}$$ $$\text{Mammal} \xleftarrow{\text{IS-A}} \text{Whale}$$ $$\text{Dog} \xrightarrow{\text{HAS-PART}} \text{Tail}$$ $$\text{Whale} \xrightarrow{\text{HAS-PART}} \text{Fluke}$$ **Task:** Compute $d(\text{Poodle}, \text{Whale})$. **Step 1 — Build the adjacency list.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Mammal} &\leftarrow \{\text{Dog}, \text{Whale}\} \\ \text{Dog} &\leftarrow \{\text{Poodle}\}, \quad \text{Dog} \rightarrow \{\text{Tail}\} \\ \text{Whale} &\rightarrow \{\text{Fluke}\} \\ \text{Poodle} &\rightarrow \emptyset \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Run BFS from `Poodle` to find shortest path to `Whale`.** $$\begin{aligned} d(\text{Poodle}, \text{Dog}) &= 1 \quad \text{(via IS-A)} \\ d(\text{Poodle}, \text{Mammal}) &= 2 \quad \text{(via Poodle → Dog → Mammal)} \\ d(\text{Poodle}, \text{Whale}) &= 3 \quad \text{(via Poodle → Dog → Mammal → Whale)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Verify by inspection.** The unique path is: Poodle $\xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}}$ Dog $\xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}}$ Mammal $\xrightarrow{\text{IS-A}}$ Whale. Length = **3 edges**. $$\boxed{d(\text{Poodle}, \text{Whale}) = 3}$$ ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Construction of a Partitioned Semantic Network To convert a first-order logic sentence into a partitioned semantic net, follow the **Woods 1975 algorithm**: **Step 1 — Identify the proposition and the entities involved.** Sentence: *"Every dog that bit a cat has a sick owner."* **Step 2 — Replace constants with variables and create a partition (box) for the universal statement.** Create a partition $P$ with variable bindings: - $\theta_1$ = the *dog* (universal) - $\theta_2$ = the *cat* (existential, exists within the antecedent) - $\theta_3$ = the *owner* (existential, exists within the consequent) **Step 3 — Encode each predicate as a node-relation structure inside the partition.** $$\begin{aligned} P : \quad & \theta_1 \xrightarrow{\text{INSTANCE-OF}} \text{Dog} \\ & \theta_2 \xrightarrow{\text{INSTANCE-OF}} \text{Cat} \\ & \theta_1 \xrightarrow{\text{AGENT}} \text{bite} \xleftarrow{\text{PATIENT}} \theta_2 \\ & \theta_3 \xrightarrow{\text{HAS-PROPERTY}} \text{sick} \\ & \theta_1 \xrightarrow{\text{HAS-OWNER}} \theta_3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Connect the partition to the outer network via the universal variable $\theta_1$.** The outer network node `Dog` is connected to partition $P$ via the edge: $$\text{Dog} \xrightarrow{\text{FOR-EVERY}} P \quad \text{with} \quad \theta_1 \text{ bound to the outer Dog.}$$ **Step 5 — Validate by re-translation to FOL.** The encoded structure is logically equivalent to: $$\forall x \big[ \text{Dog}(x) \wedge \exists y \, (\text{Cat}(y) \wedge \text{bit}(x,y)) \rightarrow \exists z \, (\text{Owner}(z,x) \wedge \text{Sick}(z)) \big]$$ > [!TIP] > **Valuation Hack:** When KTU asks you to "represent the following English statement as a semantic network", always show the **partition boundary explicitly** as a dashed box, and label the **variable bindings** ($\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots$). Half the marks are awarded for showing the partition. ## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Implementing Marker Passing for Inheritance The **marker-passing algorithm** is the canonical inference engine for semantic networks. It is **guaranteed to terminate** because the graph is finite and markers flow only along edges. **Algorithm:** $$\begin{aligned} &\text{Function INFER}(S, P): \\ &\quad \text{Initialize a marker } m_S \text{ at source node } S. \\ &\quad \text{Initialize an empty set } R = \emptyset. \\ &\quad \text{While } m_S \text{ can travel along any outgoing edge } e = (v, w, l): \\ &\quad\quad \text{If } l = \text{"IS-A"} \text{ or } l = \text{"INSTANCE-OF"} \text{ or } l = \text{"AKO":} \\ &\quad\quad\quad \text{Propagate } m_S \text{ from } v \text{ to } w; \text{ append } w \text{ to } R. \\ &\quad \text{If } l = \text{"CAN"} \text{ or } l = \text{"HAS-PROPERTY"}: \\ &\quad\quad\quad \text{If target } = P, \text{ return TRUE (property found).} \\ &\quad \text{Return FALSE (property not found).} \end{aligned}$$ **Complexity:** $O(|V| + |E|)$ for BFS-based marker passing. With index structures, it reduces to $O(\log |V|)$ per query. --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classic Semantic Network (Animal Taxonomy) The diagram below illustrates a **canonical animal taxonomy** with a few property and case-role edges, exactly as a KTU question would present it. ```mermaid graph TD entityRoot[Entity] --> physObj[Physical Object] physObj --> animal[Animal] animal --> bird[Bird] animal --> mammal[Mammal] bird --> canary[Canary] bird --> ostrich[Ostrich] bird --> sparrow[Sparrow] mammal --> dog[Dog] mammal --> whale[Whale] dog --> poodle[Poodle] poodle --> fido[Fido] canary --> tweety[Tweety] bird -- CAN --> fly[Fly] ostrich -- CAN-NOT --> fly sparrow -- HAS-PART --> wing[Wing] bird -- HAS-PART --> beak[Beak] poodle -- HAS-OWNER --> alice[Alice] whale -- LIVES-IN --> ocean[Ocean] bird -- LIVES-IN --> sky[Sky] classDef rootCls fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classCls fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#1E3A5F,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 classDef instCls fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000 classDef propCls fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class entityRoot,physObj rootCls class animal,bird,mammal,canary,ostrich,sparrow,dog,whale,poodle classCls class fido,tweety,alice instCls class fly,wing,beak,ocean,sky propCls ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Semantic Network Engine This block diagram shows how a modern semantic network processing system is structured internally — useful for KTU's "explain the architecture" type questions. ```mermaid graph LR inputNode[Input Query
