# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Taxonomy of Uncertainty Handling in Knowledge Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uncertainty in Knowledge] --> B[Randomness]
    A --> C[Vagueness]
    A --> D[Incompleteness]

    B --> B1[Probability Theory]
    B1 --> B1a[Classical Probability]
    B1 --> B1b[Kolmogorov Axioms]
    B1 --> B1c[Bayes Theorem]
    B1 --> B1d[Bayesian Networks]

    C --> C1[Fuzzy Logic]
    C1 --> C1a[Fuzzy Sets]
    C1 --> C1b[Membership Functions]
    C1 --> C1c[Fuzzy Rules]
    C1 --> C1d[Defuzzification]

    D --> D1[Possibility Theory]
    D1 --> D1a[Possibility Distribution]
    D1 --> D1b[Possibility Measure Pi]
    D1 --> D1c[Necessity Measure N]
    D1 --> D1d[Duality Property]

    B1c -.links to.-> D1
    C1 -.relates via.-> D1
```

**Diagram Interpretation**: This flowchart shows how Probability, Fuzzy Logic, and Possibility Theory are the three principal branches of uncertainty handling. Note the *dashed links* showing that Bayesian probability connects to Possibility Theory (via the Zadeh compatibility principle), and Fuzzy Logic relates to Possibility Theory (Zadeh introduced both).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Crisp Input X] --> F1[Fuzzification]
    F1 --> F2a[Membership mu A x]
    F1 --> F2b[Membership mu B x]
    F2a --> R1[Rule Base IF THEN]
    F2b --> R1
    R1 --> Agg[Rule Aggregation]
    Agg --> Def[Defuzzification]
    Def --> O1[Crisp Output Y]

    R1 --> R1a[Rule 1 IF x is A1 THEN y is B1]
    R1 --> R1b[Rule 2 IF x is A2 THEN y is B2]
    R1 --> R1c[Rule 3 IF x is A3 THEN y is B3]
```

**Diagram Interpretation**: The standard **Mamdani-type Fuzzy Inference System** has four blocks: (1) Fuzzification, (2) Rule Evaluation, (3) Aggregation, (4) Defuzzification. The crisp input $X$ enters the system and a crisp output $Y$ emerges — this is the core architecture of any fuzzy controller (e.g., the famous Mamdani steam engine controller from 1974).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Probability vs Possibility — The Sandwich Relation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Event A] --> B[Compute Necessity N of A]
    A --> C[Compute Probability P of A]
    A --> D[Compute Possibility Pi of A]

    B --> E{N is pessimistic lower bound}
    C --> F{P is precise measure}
    D --> G{Pi is optimistic upper bound}

    E --> H[Sandwich Inequality]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> H1[N of A is less than or equal to P of A is less than or equal to Pi of A]

    H1 --> I1[Case 1: N equals Pi equals 1]
    H1 --> I2[Case 2: 0 is less than N is less than Pi is less than 1]
    H1 --> I3[Case 3: N equals 0, Pi equals 1, total ignorance]
```

**Diagram Interpretation**: This block diagram visualizes the **sandwich relation** $N(A) \leq P(A) \leq \Pi(A)$. Three edge cases are highlighted:
- **Case 1**: Complete certainty ($N = \Pi = 1$).
- **Case 2**: Partial knowledge.
- **Case 3**: Total ignorance ($N = 0, \Pi = 1$).

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Bayes' Theorem Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Prior Belief P of Bi] --> BL[Likelihood P of A given Bi]
    BL --> EV[Evidence P of A]
    EV --> Bayes[Apply Bayes Theorem]
    Bayes --> PO[Posterior P of Bi given A]
    PO --> P1

    EV --> TP[Total Probability Sum over all Bj]
    TP --> EV
```

**Diagram Interpretation**: This shows the **Bayesian belief update cycle** used in expert systems and machine learning. The prior is combined with new evidence (likelihood) to produce an updated posterior, which then becomes the new prior when more evidence arrives. This iterative process is the foundation of **Bayesian learning**.

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Block-Level Architecture of a Knowledge Engineering System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    U[User Interface] --> KB[Knowledge Base]
    KB --> KB1[Facts and Rules]
    KB --> KB2[Fuzzy Rule Sets]
    KB --> KB3[Probabilistic Beliefs]
    KB --> KB4[Possibility Distributions]

    KB --> IE[Inference Engine]
    IE --> IE1[Forward Chaining]
    IE --> IE2[Backward Chaining]
    IE --> IE3[Fuzzy Reasoning]
    IE --> IE4[Bayesian Updating]

    IE --> RE[Reasoning and Explanation Module]
    RE --> U

    KB -.feeds.-> IUnc[Uncertainty Handler]
    IUnc --> IUnc1[Probability Module]
    IUnc --> IUnc2[Fuzzy Module]
    IUnc --> IUnc3[Possibility Module]
    IUnc1 --> IE
    IUnc2 --> IE
    IUnc3 --> IE
```

**Diagram Interpretation**: A complete knowledge engineering system integrates all three uncertainty frameworks through a dedicated **Uncertainty Handler** block, which dispatches to the appropriate module (Probability, Fuzzy, or Possibility) based on the type of knowledge being processed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Tip for KTU Viva**: Be ready to *verbally explain* any of these diagrams. Examiners often draw a blank box and ask "what would you put here?" — answer with reference to the appropriate uncertainty module.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between Probability Theory and Fuzzy Logic. Give one example where each is applied."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Aspect | Probability Theory | Fuzzy Logic |
|---|---|---|
| **Type of Uncertainty** | Handles stochastic randomness | Handles vagueness / imprecision |
| **Range** | $P(A) \in [0,1]$ on events | $\mu(x) \in [0,1]$ on elements |
| **Question Answered** | "How likely is $A$?" | "To what degree does $x$ belong to $A$?" |
| **Originator** | Kolmogorov (1933) | Zadeh (1965) |

- **Probability Example**: Predicting the failure of a component in the next 1000 hours ($P = 0.02$).
- **Fuzzy Logic Example**: A washing machine controller using rules like "If load is *heavy*, then wash time is *long*."

> *Award 1 mark for distinction table, 1 mark for probability example, 1 mark for fuzzy example.*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"State the Kolmogorov axioms of probability and explain the necessity of each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

1. **Non-Negativity**: $P(A) \geq 0$ for every event $A$.
   *Necessity*: Probabilities represent physical likelihoods and cannot be negative.
2. **Normalization**: $P(\Omega) = 1$, where $\Omega$ is the sample space.
   *Necessity*: Ensures the scale is bounded in $[0, 1]$; the certain event has probability 1.
3. **Countable Additivity**: $P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)$ for mutually exclusive events.
   *Necessity*: Allows decomposition of complex events into simple disjoint components.

> *Award 1 mark per axiom with a sentence of justification.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation**: Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Students must answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B. Partial answers between A and B are not permitted.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"Explain in detail the Probability Theory framework as applied to Knowledge Engineering. Your answer must include: (a) the Kolmogorov axioms with a worked derivation of Bayes' theorem, and (b) a complete Bayesian inference example for a medical diagnosis expert system with three diseases."**

#### Part (a) — Kolmogorov Axioms and Bayes' Theorem Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: State the Axioms (2 Marks)**

The probability function $P: \mathcal{F} \to [0, 1]$ satisfies:

1. **Axiom of Non-Negativity**: $\forall A \in \mathcal{F}, \; P(A) \geq 0$
2. **Axiom of Normalization**: $P(\Omega) = 1$
3. **Axiom of Countable Additivity**: For mutually exclusive events $A_1, A_2, \ldots$:

$$
P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} P(A_i)
$$

**Step 2: State the Conditional Probability Definition (1 Mark)**

For $P(B) > 0$:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

**Step 3: Derive Bayes' Theorem (4 Marks)**

Starting from the conditional probability definition:
$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)} \implies P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Substitute into $P(A \mid B)$:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Generalize using Total Probability Theorem for a partition $\{B_1, \ldots, B_n\}$ of $\Omega$:

$$P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Substituting this denominator yields the general form:

$$
P(B_i \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B_i) \cdot P(B_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_j) \cdot P(B_j)} \quad \text{(1 mark for final formula)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Medical Diagnosis Example (7 Marks)

**Problem Statement (1 Mark)**:
A medical expert system has three possible diseases $D_1$ (Flu), $D_2$ (COVID), $D_3$ (Allergy) with prior probabilities based on seasonal data:
- $P(D_1) = 0.4, \; P(D_2) = 0.35, \; P(D_3) = 0.25$

A fever symptom $F$ has the following likelihoods:
- $P(F \mid D_1) = 0.9, \; P(F \mid D_2) = 0.8, \; P(F \mid D_3) = 0.1$

A patient presents with fever. Compute the posterior probabilities.

**Step 1: Compute Evidence $P(F)$ (2 Marks)**

$$
P(F) = (0.9 \times 0.4) + (0.8 \times 0.35) + (0.1 \times 0.25) = 0.36 + 0.28 + 0.025 = 0.665
$$

**Step 2: Apply Bayes' Theorem for Each Disease (3 Marks)**

$$
P(D_1 \mid F) = \frac{0.9 \times 0.4}{0.665} = \frac{0.36}{0.665} \approx 0.5414
$$

$$
P(D_2 \mid F) = \frac{0.8 \times 0.35}{0.665} = \frac{0.28}{0.665} \approx 0.4211
$$

$$
P(D_3 \mid F) = \frac{0.1 \times 0.25}{0.665} = \frac{0.025}{0.665} \approx 0.0376
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation and Verification (1 Mark)**

Verification: $0.5414 + 0.4211 + 0.0376 = 1.0001 \approx 1.00 \checkmark$ (small rounding error)

**Interpretation**: The expert system would now recommend Flu as the most likely diagnosis (54.14%), followed by COVID (42.11%), and Allergy as the least likely (3.76%). The expert system uses these updated beliefs to determine which follow-up tests to suggest.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Forgetting to compute the evidence $P(F)$ as a *sum* over all hypotheses — this costs 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Confusing prior with likelihood — remember $P(D_i)$ is prior, $P(F \mid D_i)$ is likelihood, $P(D_i \mid F)$ is posterior.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Not verifying that posterior probabilities sum to 1 — the KTU examiner deducts 1 mark for this.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Using Bayes' theorem for *non-partition* events — if events are not mutually exclusive and exhaustive, the formula is invalid.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**"Discuss the frameworks of Fuzzy Logic and Possibility Theory in handling uncertainty in Knowledge Engineering. Your answer must include: (a) the definition of fuzzy sets with all three standard operations on a worked numerical example, and (b) the relationship between Possibility and Necessity measures with the sandwich inequality $N(A) \leq P(A) \leq \Pi(A)$."**

#### Part (a) — Fuzzy Sets and Operations (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define a Fuzzy Set (2 Marks)**

A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ in universe $X$ is defined as:
$$\tilde{A} = \{(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X\}, \quad \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \in [0,1]$$

where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ is the *membership function* assigning the degree of belonging of $x$ to $\tilde{A}$.

**Step 2: State the Three Standard Operations (2 Marks)**

For fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$:

- **Union** (OR): $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$
- **Intersection** (AND): $\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$
- **Complement** (NOT): $\mu_{\overline{\tilde{A}}}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$

**Step 3: Worked Numerical Example (3 Marks)**

Let $X = \{a, b, c, d\}$ with:
- $\tilde{A} = \{(a, 0.3), (b, 0.8), (c, 0.5), (d, 0.1)\}$
- $\tilde{B} = \{(a, 0.7), (b, 0.4), (c, 0.6), (d, 0.9)\}$

**Union** ($\max$):
- $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{(a, 0.7), (b, 0.8), (c, 0.6), (d, 0.9)\}$ — *(1 mark)*

**Intersection** ($\min$):
- $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \{(a, 0.3), (b, 0.4), (c, 0.5), (d, 0.1)\}$ — *(1 mark)*

**Complement of $\tilde{A}$**:
- $\overline{\tilde{A}} = \{(a, 0.7), (b, 0.2), (c, 0.5), (d, 0.9)\}$ — *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Possibility and Necessity Relationship (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define the Possibility Measure (1 Mark)**

Given a possibility distribution $\pi: X \to [0,1]$ with $\sup_{x \in X} \pi(x) = 1$:
$$\Pi(A) = \sup_{x \in A} \pi(x)$$

**Step 2: Define the Necessity Measure (1 Mark)**

$$N(A) = 1 - \Pi(\overline{A}) = 1 - \sup_{x \notin A} \pi(x)$$

**Step 3: Prove the Duality $N(A) \leq \Pi(A)$ (2 Marks)**

We show: $1 - \sup_{x \notin A} \pi(x) \leq \sup_{x \in A} \pi(x)$

Equivalently: $1 \leq \sup_{x \in A} \pi(x) + \sup_{x \notin A} \pi(x)$

Since $X = A \cup \overline{A}$ and $\sup_{x \in X} \pi(x) = 1$:
$$\sup_{x \in A} \pi(x) + \sup_{x \notin A} \pi(x) \geq \sup_{x \in A} \pi(x) = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 4: State the Sandwich Inequality (1 Mark)**

For any event $A$:
$$N(A) \leq P(A) \leq \Pi(A)$$

**Step 5: Numerical Illustration (2 Marks)**

Let $X = \{x_1, x_2, x_3\}$ with possibility distribution $\pi = (0.6, 0.9, 0.1)$ and probability distribution $p = (0.5, 0.3, 0.2)$.

For $A = \{x_1, x_2\}$:

- $\Pi(A) = \max(0.6, 0.9) = 0.9$
- $\Pi(\overline{A}) = \Pi(\{x_3\}) = 0.1$
- $N(A) = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9$
- $P(A) = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8$

Verification of sandwich: $N(A) = 0.9 \leq P(A) = 0.8 \leq \Pi(A) = 0.9$ — holds in this case with $P(A) = 0.8$ between the bounds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B)**:
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Confusing fuzzy set operations with classical set operations — fuzzy uses $\max$ and $\min$, not OR/AND in the Boolean sense.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting the normalization condition $\sup \pi(x) = 1$ for possibility distributions — this is a defining property worth 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Stating $N(A) \leq P(A) \leq \Pi(A)$ without proving the duality relation — KTU requires the derivation.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Writing the complement of a fuzzy set as $0$ for all elements (Boolean thinking) — it must be $1 - \mu(x)$ for each $x$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist**

### Probability Theory Essentials
- ☐ **Kolmogorov's 3 Axioms**: Non-negativity, Normalization ($P(\Omega) = 1$), Countable Additivity.
- ☐ **Bayes' Theorem**: $P(B_i \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)}{\sum_j P(A \mid B_j) P(B_j)}$ — the most important formula.
- ☐ **Total Probability**: $P(A) = \sum_i P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)$ when $\{B_i\}$ partitions $\Omega$.
- ☐ **Independence vs Mutual Exclusivity**: Independent means $P(A \cap B) = P(A)P(B)$; mutually exclusive means $P(A \cap B) = 0$. These are **different** concepts.

### Fuzzy Logic Essentials
- ☐ **Membership Function**: $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \in [0, 1]$ — the heart of fuzzy sets.
- ☐ **Three Operators**: Union = max, Intersection = min, Complement = $1 - \mu$.
- ☐ **Alternative Operators**: Algebraic product ($xy$) for AND, Algebraic sum ($x + y - xy$) for OR.
- ☐ **Hedges**: "Very" squares the membership; "Somewhat" takes the square root.
- ☐ **Defuzzification**: Centroid method is the most common.

### Possibility Theory Essentials
- ☐ **Possibility Distribution**: $\pi: X \to [0,1]$ with $\sup \pi(x) = 1$.
- ☐ **Possibility Measure**: $\Pi(A) = \sup_{x \in A} \pi(x)$ — *optimistic* upper bound.
- ☐ **Necessity Measure**: $N(A) = 1 - \Pi(\overline{A})$ — *pessimistic* lower bound.
- ☐ **Duality**: $N(A) \leq \Pi(A)$ always.
- ☐ **Sandwich**: $N(A) \leq P(A) \leq \Pi(A)$ — probability lies between necessity and possibility.
- ☐ **Compatibility Principle** (Zadeh): $C(\pi, p) = \sum_i \pi_i p_i$ measures consistency.

### Exam Strategy Quick Tips
- ☐ Always draw a **comparison table** when asked to differentiate.
- ☐ Show **derivation steps** in Bayes' theorem — partial credit is given for methodology.
- ☐ State the **normalization condition** explicitly when working with possibility distributions.
- ☐ In numerical problems, **verify posterior probabilities sum to 1** — this catches arithmetic errors.
- ☐ Use **mermaid-style diagrams** in your answer sheets to illustrate fuzzy inference pipelines.
- ☐ Remember the KTU 2024 weightage: this module typically contributes **15-20%** of the end-semester paper.

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Probability Theory, Fuzzy Logic, Possibility Theory**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reasoning Under Uncertainty: Bayesian Networks, Probabilistic Reasoning, Fuzzy Inference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT521 - KNOWLEDGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reasoning Under Uncertainty: Bayesian Networks, Probabilistic Reasoning, Fuzzy Inference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Reasoning Under Uncertainty: Bayesian Networks, Probabilistic Reasoning & Fuzzy Inference

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Reasoning Under Uncertainty"?

In classical (Boolean) logic, every proposition is either strictly **TRUE** or strictly **FALSE**. Real-world engineering and AI systems, however, almost never enjoy such luxury. Sensor noise, incomplete data, linguistic imprecision (e.g., *"the room is somewhat warm"*), and probabilistic events all force an intelligent system to reason with **degrees of belief** rather than absolutes. This is termed **Reasoning Under Uncertainty**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Reasoning Under Uncertainty is the sub-field of Knowledge Engineering that provides formal mathematical frameworks for representing, propagating, and updating the degree of confidence (belief) in propositions when the available evidence is incomplete, noisy, or ambiguous.*

Two dominant mathematical frameworks are prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus for this module:

1. **Probabilistic Reasoning (Bayesian Networks)** — based on classical probability theory and Bayes' theorem.
2. **Fuzzy Inference (Fuzzy Logic Systems)** — based on fuzzy set theory introduced by Lotfi Zadeh (1965).

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cloudy Morning Forecast"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine you wake up in Kerala during monsoon season and see dark clouds. You *believe* (but are not 100% certain) that it will rain.
> - A **probabilistic** reasoner would state: *"Given dark clouds, P(rain) = 0.75."*
> - A **fuzzy** reasoner would say: *"Cloud cover is 0.8 HIGH, temperature is 0.6 WARM, therefore comfort level is 0.4 LOW."*
>
> Probability handles **randomness**; Fuzzy logic handles **vagueness/linguistic imprecision**.

---

### 1.3 Bayesian Networks — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayesian Network (BN):**
> A Bayesian Network is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) in which:
> - **Nodes** represent random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$.
> - **Directed Edges** represent direct probabilistic (causal) dependencies between variables.
> - Each node $X_i$ is associated with a **Conditional Probability Table (CPT)** $P(X_i \;\vert\; \text{Parents}(X_i))$.
>
> The joint probability distribution is factorised as:
> $$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P\bigl(X_i \;\big\vert\; \text{Parents}(X_i)\bigr)$$

### 1.4 Probabilistic Reasoning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Probabilistic Reasoning:**
> A formal methodology for updating the probability of hypotheses as new evidence arrives. Its cornerstone is **Bayes' Theorem**, which mathematically inverts conditional probabilities.

### 1.5 Fuzzy Inference — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy Inference System (FIS):**
> A computing framework based on the concepts of **fuzzy sets**, **linguistic variables**, and **if-then fuzzy rules** that maps one or more crisp inputs to one or more crisp outputs through the stages of **Fuzzification → Rule Evaluation → Defuzzification**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical / Standard Metrics used in this Module:**
> - **Probability value** ranges in the closed interval $[0, 1]$.
> - **Fuzzy Membership value** $\mu_A(x)$ also ranges in $[0, 1]$.
> - **Possibility vs. Probability**: Possibility (fuzzy) is non-additive ($\sum \mu \neq 1$); probability is additive ($\sum P = 1$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Uncertainty in AI Systems

| # | Source | Description | Typical Tool Used |
|---|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Incomplete Information** | Some relevant evidence is unavailable | Bayesian Networks |
| 2 | **Noisy Sensor Data** | Measurement errors corrupt inputs | Probabilistic filters |
| 3 | **Vague / Linguistic Inputs** | "Hot", "Fast", "Loud" — no crisp boundary | Fuzzy Logic |
| 4 | **Randomness** | Coin toss, weather | Probability Theory |
| 5 | **Conflicting Evidence** | Multiple experts disagree | Dempster-Shafer Theory |

---

## 2.2 Bayesian Theorem — The Logical Heart

### 2.2.1 Conditional Probability

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \quad \text{where } P(B) > 0$$

### 2.2.2 Bayes' Theorem (Posterior Calculation)

$$P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$$

Where:

- $P(H)$ = **Prior** probability of hypothesis $H$ (before evidence).
- $P(E \mid H)$ = **Likelihood** of observing evidence $E$ given $H$.
- $P(E)$ = **Marginal** probability of evidence (normalising constant).
- $P(H \mid E)$ = **Posterior** probability of $H$ after observing $E$.

### 2.2.3 Bayes' Theorem for Multiple Hypotheses (Total Probability)

For mutually exclusive and exhaustive hypotheses $H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_n$:

$$P(H_i \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)}$$

### 2.2.4 Chain Rule of Probabilities

$$P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2 \mid X_1) \cdot P(X_3 \mid X_1, X_2) \cdots P(X_n \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{n-1})$$

### 2.2.5 Conditional Independence

$X$ is conditionally independent of $Y$ given $Z$ if:

$$P(X, Y \mid Z) = P(X \mid Z) \cdot P(Y \mid Z)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Conditional Independence" is the key to tractable Bayesian Networks:**
> Without it, computing $P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{30})$ would require $2^{30}-1$ entries — an astronomical number. Conditional independence lets the network **factorise** the joint into a small product of CPTs.

---

## 2.3 Fuzzy Set Theory Essentials

### 2.3.1 Fuzzy Set Definition

A fuzzy set $A$ on universal set $X$ is defined by a **membership function** $\mu_A : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$.

$$A = \{\,(x, \mu_A(x)) \;\mid\; x \in X\,\}$$

### 2.3.2 Standard Membership Function Forms

| Function | Equation | Use Case |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Triangular | $\mu(x) = \max\!\left(0, \min\!\left(\frac{x-a}{b-a}, \frac{c-x}{c-b}\right)\right)$ | Piecewise linear rules |
| Trapezoidal | $\mu(x) = \max\!\left(0, \min\!\left(\frac{x-a}{b-a}, 1, \frac{d-x}{d-c}\right)\right)$ | Plateau regions |
| Gaussian | $\mu(x) = e^{-\frac{(x-c)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}}$ | Smooth bell-shaped fuzziness |

### 2.3.3 Fuzzy Set Operations

For two fuzzy sets $A$ and $B$ on $X$:

| Operation | Membership Formula |
|-----------|--------------------|
| Union ($A \cup B$) | $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max\!\bigl(\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(x)\bigr)$ |
| Intersection ($A \cap B$) | $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min\!\bigl(\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(x)\bigr)$ |
| Complement ($\bar{A}$) | $\mu_{\bar{A}}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$ |

---

## 2.4 Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) — Operational Flow

The Mamdani FIS operates in **four sequential stages**:

1. **Fuzzification** — Convert crisp inputs to membership degrees.
2. **Rule Evaluation** — Apply IF-THEN rules using fuzzy operators (typically MIN for AND, MAX for OR).
3. **Aggregation** — Combine outputs of all rules into a single fuzzy set.
4. **Defuzzification** — Convert the aggregated fuzzy set into a crisp output.

### 2.4.1 Defuzzification Methods

| Method | Formula | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| **Centroid (COG / COA)** | $x^{*} = \dfrac{\int x\,\mu(x)\,dx}{\int \mu(x)\,dx}$ | Most common; centre of gravity |
| **Bisector** | $x^{*}$ such that $\int_{a}^{x^{*}}\!\mu(x)\,dx = \int_{x^{*}}^{b}\!\mu(x)\,dx$ | Splits area into two equal halves |
| **Mean of Maximum (MOM)** | $x^{*} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} x_i}{N}$ | Average of points where $\mu$ is maximum |
| **Smallest of Maximum (SOM)** | $x^{*} = \min\{x_i : \mu(x_i) = \mu_{\max}\}$ | Leftmost maximum |
| **Largest of Maximum (LOM)** | $x^{*} = \max\{x_i : \mu(x_i) = \mu_{\max}\}$ | Rightmost maximum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> - **Bayesian Networks**: Medical diagnosis (QMR-DT), spam filtering (Naïve Bayes), speech recognition, fault diagnosis in industrial systems, gene regulatory networks.
> - **Fuzzy Inference**: Air-conditioner temperature control, washing machines, automatic transmission in cars, camera autofocus, stock-market prediction, traffic-light timing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bayes' Theorem (from First Principles)

We start with the **definition of conditional probability** for two events $A$ and $B$ where $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

By symmetry of intersection, $P(A \cap B) = P(B \cap A)$. Therefore:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Equating (i) and (ii) — both expressions equal $P(A \cap B)$:

$$P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

Rearranging to isolate $P(A \mid B)$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

Substituting $A \to H$ (hypothesis) and $B \to E$ (evidence) for clarity:

$$\boxed{P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}}$$

This is the classical form. If the evidence $E$ can be produced by $n$ mutually exclusive causes $H_1, \ldots, H_n$, the law of total probability gives:

$$P(E) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)$$

Substituting back yields the **extended form** used in Bayesian Network inference:

$$\boxed{P(H_i \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}{\sum\limits_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Medical Diagnosis Bayesian Network

> **Problem Context:** A patient is tested for a rare disease. The disease affects **1 %** of the population ($P(D) = 0.01$). The test correctly identifies the disease in sick people 95 % of the time — $P(+ \mid D) = 0.95$ (sensitivity). The test incorrectly flags healthy people 4 % of the time — $P(+ \mid \neg D) = 0.04$ (false positive rate).
>
> **Question:** If a patient tests **positive**, what is the probability they actually have the disease? Compute $P(D \mid +)$.

### Step-by-step Analytical Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the events and priors.**

- Hypothesis $D$: patient has disease. $P(D) = 0.01$.
- $\neg D$: patient is healthy. $P(\neg D) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$.
- Evidence $+$: test gives positive result.

**Step 2 — Identify the likelihoods.**

- $P(+ \mid D) = 0.95$.
- $P(+ \mid \neg D) = 0.04$.

**Step 3 — Apply the law of total probability to compute $P(+)$.**

$$P(+) = P(+ \mid D) \cdot P(D) + P(+ \mid \neg D) \cdot P(\neg D)$$

$$P(+) = (0.95)(0.01) + (0.04)(0.99)$$

$$P(+) = 0.0095 + 0.0396$$

$$P(+) = 0.0491$$

**Step 4 — Apply Bayes' theorem to find the posterior.**

$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{P(+ \mid D) \cdot P(D)}{P(+)}$$

$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{(0.95)(0.01)}{0.0491}$$

$$P(D \mid +) = \frac{0.0095}{0.0491}$$

$$\boxed{P(D \mid +) \approx 0.1935 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 19.35\%}$$

### Interpretation / Engineering Insight:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Even with a 95 % accurate test, a positive result means only about **19.35 %** chance of actually having the disease. This counter-intuitive result occurs because the disease is very rare (low prior). This is the classic **base-rate fallacy** and is the single most important reason Bayesian reasoning is vital in medical AI.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Bayesian Network Inference (Asia-style Toy Network)

Consider the classic *Lung-Cancer* Bayesian Network:

$$\text{Smoking} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Lung Cancer} \;\rightarrow\; \text{X-Ray Result}$$
$$\text{Smoking} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Bronchitis} \;\rightarrow\; \text{X-Ray Result}$$

The conditional probability tables are:

- $P(S = \text{yes}) = 0.30$.
- $P(L = \text{yes} \mid S = \text{yes}) = 0.10$, $P(L = \text{yes} \mid S = \text{no}) = 0.01$.
- $P(X = \text{pos} \mid L = \text{yes}, B = \text{yes}) = 0.90$.
- $P(X = \text{pos} \mid L = \text{yes}, B = \text{no}) = 0.70$.
- $P(X = \text{pos} \mid L = \text{no}, B = \text{yes}) = 0.30$.
- $P(X = \text{pos} \mid L = \text{no}, B = \text{no}) = 0.05$.
- $P(B = \text{yes} \mid S = \text{yes}) = 0.60$, $P(B = \text{yes} \mid S = \text{no}) = 0.05$.

**Compute the joint factorisation $P(S, L, B, X)$ using the chain rule on the DAG.**

$$P(S, L, B, X) = P(S) \cdot P(L \mid S) \cdot P(B \mid S) \cdot P(X \mid L, B)$$

This decomposition (vs. the full $2^4 - 1 = 15$ independent parameters required by a naïve joint table) reduces the required parameters significantly — illustrating the **sparse modelling power** of BNs.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Centroid Defuzzification (Mamdani FIS)

Suppose we have an aggregated output fuzzy set with two triangular regions:

- Region 1: Triangle with vertices at $(x, \mu) = (0, 0), (2, 1), (4, 0)$ — clipped at $\mu = 0.6$.
- Region 2: Triangle with vertices at $(x, \mu) = (3, 0), (5, 1), (7, 0)$ — clipped at $\mu = 0.8$.

**Compute the crisp output $x^{*}$ using the centroid (COG) method.**

### Step 1 — Write the clipped membership equations.

For Region 1 (clipped triangle, area $\frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}_{\text{clipped}}$):

$$\mu_1(x) = \begin{cases} 0.6 \cdot \dfrac{x - 0}{2 - 0} = 0.3x & 0 \le x \le 2 \\[4pt] 0.6 \cdot \dfrac{4 - x}{4 - 2} = 0.3(4 - x) & 2 \le x \le 4 \\[4pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

For Region 2 (clipped triangle):

$$\mu_2(x) = \begin{cases} 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{x - 3}{5 - 3} = 0.4(x - 3) & 3 \le x \le 5 \\[4pt] 0.8 \cdot \dfrac{7 - x}{7 - 5} = 0.4(7 - x) & 5 \le x \le 7 \\[4pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the combined numerator $\int x\,\mu(x)\,dx$.

Break the integral into segments. Using symmetry of triangular regions:

$$\int_0^4 x \cdot \mu_1(x)\,dx = 0.6 \cdot \underbrace{\int_0^4 x \cdot \mu_{\text{tri}}(x)\,dx}_{\text{unit triangle}} = 0.6 \cdot 4 = 2.4$$

Similarly:

$$\int_3^7 x \cdot \mu_2(x)\,dx = 0.8 \cdot 7 = 5.6$$

Total numerator:

$$N = 2.4 + 5.6 = 8.0$$

### Step 3 — Compute the combined denominator $\int \mu(x)\,dx$ (areas of the clipped triangles).

$$A_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 0.6 = 1.2$$

$$A_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4 \cdot 0.8 = 1.6$$

$$D = A_1 + A_2 = 1.2 + 1.6 = 2.8$$

### Step 4 — Compute the centroid.

$$x^{*} = \frac{N}{D} = \frac{8.0}{2.8}$$

$$\boxed{x^{*} \approx 2.857}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Criterion | Bayesian Networks | Fuzzy Inference Systems |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Theoretical Basis | Probability Theory (Kolmogorov axioms) | Fuzzy Set Theory (Zadeh, 1965) |
| Handles | **Randomness / Stochastic uncertainty** | **Vagueness / Linguistic imprecision** |
| Underlying Logic | Multi-valued but additive | Multi-valued, non-additive |
| Knowledge Representation | DAG + CPTs | IF-THEN linguistic rules + Membership functions |
| Inference Mechanism | Variable Elimination, Junction Tree, MCMC | Max-Min composition, Mamdani, Sugeno |
| Output Type | Probability distribution over variables | Crisp scalar (after defuzzification) |
| Best Suited For | Diagnostic, predictive systems with numeric data | Control systems with expert linguistic rules |
| Combined Used In | Bayesian-Fuzzy hybrids, expert systems, ADAS | Washing machines, AC controllers, ABS braking |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bayesian Network Topology — "Sprinkler-Cloudy-Rain-Wet Grass" Classic Toy Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeC[Cloudy] --> nodeS[Sprinkler]
    nodeC[Cloudy] --> nodeR[Rain]
    nodeS[Sprinkler] --> nodeW[WetGrass]
    nodeR[Rain] --> nodeW[WetGrass]
    nodeC --- nodeCP["P(C)=0.5"]
    nodeS --- nodeSP["P(S|C) CPT"]
    nodeR --- nodeRP["P(R|C) CPT"]
    nodeW --- nodeWP["P(W|S,R) CPT"]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- `Cloudy` is the root node (no parents).
- `Sprinkler` and `Rain` are conditionally independent given `Cloudy`.
- `WetGrass` has two parents, so its CPT is indexed by 4 combinations.
- The full joint distribution factorises as:

$$P(C, S, R, W) = P(C)\,P(S \mid C)\,P(R \mid C)\,P(W \mid S, R)$$

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Crisp Input 1 (x1)"] --> fuzz1["Fuzzification MF1"]
    inputB["Crisp Input 2 (x2)"] --> fuzz2["Fuzzification MF2"]
    fuzz1 --> rulebase["Fuzzy Rule Base (IF-THEN Knowledge)"]
    fuzz2 --> rulebase
    rulebase --> inference["Inference Engine (Min-Max Composition)"]
    inference --> combine["Aggregation (Union of Outputs)"]
    combine --> defuzz["Defuzzification (Centroid / MOM / Bisector)"]
    defuzz --> crispout["Crisp Control Output (y*)"]
```

**Stage-by-stage mapping:**

| Stage | Block Name in Diagram | Operation |
|-------|----------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Fuzzification MF1 / MF2 | Crisp $x \to \mu(x)$ |
| 2 | Fuzzy Rule Base | Stores linguistic IF-THEN rules |
| 3 | Inference Engine | Computes firing strength of each rule |
| 4 | Aggregation | Combines rule outputs (MAX operator) |
| 5 | Defuzzification | Converts fuzzy set $\to$ crisp $y^{*}$ |

---

## 4.3 Bayesian Inference Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    prior["Prior Knowledge P(H)"] --> bayes["Bayesian Updating Engine"]
    evidence["New Evidence E"] --> bayes
    likelihood["Likelihood P(E|H)"] --> bayes
    bayes --> normalise["Marginalisation P(E)"]
    normalise --> posterior["Posterior P(H|E)"]
    posterior --> decision["Decision / Prediction"]
```

---

## 4.4 Variable Elimination — Inference Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    qA["Query Node Q"] --> elimA["Eliminate Evidence E"]
    elimA --> bucket["Bucket Creation per Variable"]
    bucket --> sumprod["Sum-Product Marginalisation"]
    sumprod --> newa["New Factor Construction"]
    newa --> final["P(Q|E) Normalised"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Bayesian Network. Explain the role of the Conditional Probability Table (CPT) with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Bayesian Network is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) where each node is a random variable and each edge represents a direct conditional dependency. Each node is annotated with a **Conditional Probability Table (CPT)** that quantifies the effect of its parents on it.

*Example:* In a network `Cloudy → Rain → WetGrass`, the CPT of `WetGrass` specifies $P(W \mid R, \neg R)$ — i.e., probability of wet grass given whether it rained. Without the CPT, the structure alone conveys no numerical information. *(3 marks for definition + role + example.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Fuzzy Logic and Probability Theory. Why is fuzzy logic preferred for control applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Probability Theory | Fuzzy Logic |
|--------|---------------------|--------------|
| Meaning of value | Likelihood of occurrence | Degree of membership |
| Sum rule | $\sum P = 1$ | $\sum \mu$ need not equal 1 |
| Handles | Randomness | Vagueness/Linguistic imprecision |

Fuzzy logic is preferred for control applications (e.g., AC, washing machines) because expert knowledge is naturally expressed in linguistic rules ("IF temperature is HIGH, THEN fan speed is FAST"), and the inference is computationally simple (min-max operations), making it ideal for real-time embedded controllers. *(3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** State and prove **Bayes' Theorem**. Discuss its significance in reasoning under uncertainty. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory has three machines $M_1, M_2, M_3$ producing 30 %, 45 %, and 25 % of the total output respectively. The defect rates are 2 %, 3 %, and 2 % respectively. If a randomly selected item is **defective**, find the probability that it came from $M_1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Statement:** For events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

**Proof:** From the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \quad \text{and} \quad P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A)}$$

Rearranging the second equation:

$$P(A \cap B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

Substituting into the first:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

Hence proved. **Significance:** Bayes' theorem allows the probability of a hypothesis to be updated as new evidence becomes available — a cornerstone of modern AI, expert systems, and machine learning. *(7 marks: 2 for statement, 3 for proof, 2 for significance.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given data:**

- $P(M_1) = 0.30, \; P(M_2) = 0.45, \; P(M_3) = 0.25$.
- $P(D \mid M_1) = 0.02, \; P(D \mid M_2) = 0.03, \; P(D \mid M_3) = 0.02$.

**Step 1 — Compute $P(D)$ using the law of total probability:**

$$P(D) = P(D \mid M_1) P(M_1) + P(D \mid M_2) P(M_2) + P(D \mid M_3) P(M_3)$$

$$P(D) = (0.02)(0.30) + (0.03)(0.45) + (0.02)(0.25)$$

$$P(D) = 0.0060 + 0.0135 + 0.0050 = 0.0245$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bayes' theorem:**

$$P(M_1 \mid D) = \frac{P(D \mid M_1) \cdot P(M_1)}{P(D)} = \frac{(0.02)(0.30)}{0.0245}$$

$$P(M_1 \mid D) = \frac{0.0060}{0.0245}$$

$$\boxed{P(M_1 \mid D) \approx 0.2449 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 24.49\%}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for total probability calculation, 2 for final Bayes' application.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Mamdani Fuzzy Inference System** with a neat block diagram. List and briefly describe any **three defuzzification methods**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a two-input one-output fuzzy controller for an **air conditioner** with inputs *Temperature* (Low/Medium/High) and *Humidity* (Low/High) and output *Fan Speed* (Slow/Medium/Fast). Given crisp inputs $T = 28^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $H = 65\%$, where $\mu_{\text{Medium}}(28) = 0.6$ and $\mu_{\text{High}}(65) = 0.7$, evaluate the rules and find the crisp fan speed using the **centroid method**, assuming clipped triangular output membership functions at 0.6 and 0.7. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

The **Mamdani FIS** consists of four blocks:

1. **Fuzzification Module** — converts crisp inputs into membership values.
2. **Knowledge Base / Rule Base** — stores IF-THEN linguistic rules.
3. **Inference Engine** — applies fuzzy operators (MIN for AND, MAX for OR) and the implication method (MIN by default).
4. **Defuzzification Module** — converts aggregated fuzzy output into a crisp value.

**Three Defuzzification Methods:**

- **Centroid (COG):** Computes the centre of gravity of the aggregated fuzzy set: $x^{*} = \dfrac{\int x\,\mu(x)\,dx}{\int \mu(x)\,dx}$. Most widely used due to smoothness.
- **Mean of Maximum (MOM):** Averages all $x$-values where the membership function attains its maximum. Suitable for peaked outputs.
- **Bisector:** Finds the $x$-value that splits the area under $\mu(x)$ into two equal halves. *(7 marks: 3 for block diagram, 4 for defuzzification methods.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Rule Base (assumed standard):**

- **R1:** IF Temperature is Medium AND Humidity is Low THEN Fan Speed is Slow.
- **R2:** IF Temperature is Medium AND Humidity is High THEN Fan Speed is Medium.

**Step 1 — Compute rule firing strengths:**

$$\alpha_1 = \min\!\bigl(\mu_{\text{Medium}}(28),\, \mu_{\text{Low}}(65)\bigr)$$

Assuming $\mu_{\text{Low}}(65) = 0.3$ (since $\mu_{\text{High}}(65) = 0.7$):

$$\alpha_1 = \min(0.6,\, 0.3) = 0.3$$

$$\alpha_2 = \min\!\bigl(\mu_{\text{Medium}}(28),\, \mu_{\text{High}}(65)\bigr) = \min(0.6,\, 0.7) = 0.6$$

**Step 2 — Clip the output triangles:**

- Slow triangle clipped to height $0.3$.
- Medium triangle clipped to height $0.6$.

**Step 3 — Compute aggregated numerator and denominator:**

For Slow (centred at, say, $x = 30$ with width $20$):

$$N_1 = 0.3 \cdot 30 = 9.0, \quad A_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 20 \cdot 0.3 = 3.0$$

For Medium (centred at $x = 50$ with width $20$):

$$N_2 = 0.6 \cdot 50 = 30.0, \quad A_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 20 \cdot 0.6 = 6.0$$

**Step 4 — Centroid:**

$$x^{*} = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{A_1 + A_2} = \frac{9.0 + 30.0}{3.0 + 6.0} = \frac{39.0}{9.0}$$

$$\boxed{x^{*} \approx 4.33 \;\; \text{(Fan Speed level, normalised)}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for rule firing, 2 for clipping, 3 for centroid computation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in this Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the marginalising denominator** in Bayes' theorem — students compute $\frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{\text{something wrong}}$ and lose 3–4 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing probability with possibility** — saying "fuzzy logic handles probability" is a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the chain-rule factorisation step** when explaining Bayesian Networks. The examiner expects the explicit product $\prod P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$.
> 4. **In defuzzification problems, failing to clip the triangles** before integration. Unclipped triangles will yield a wrong centroid.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of conditional independence** when justifying why a Bayesian Network can factorise the joint — this is worth at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up Sugeno and Mamdani** in fuzzy inference answers. Always clarify which FIS type you are using.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Reasoning Under Uncertainty**
>
> ✅ Uncertainty arises from randomness, vagueness, incompleteness, and noisy evidence.
>
> ✅ **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$.
>
> ✅ **Bayesian Network = DAG + CPTs** — edges encode conditional dependence, CPTs encode numerical strengths.
>
> ✅ **Joint distribution factorisation:** $P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$.
>
> ✅ **Conditional Independence** is the key factor that makes Bayesian Networks tractable.
>
> ✅ **Inference Methods:** Exact (Variable Elimination, Junction Tree) and Approximate (MCMC, Rejection Sampling, Likelihood Weighting).
>
> ✅ **Fuzzy Set $A$:** defined by $\mu_A : X \to [0, 1]$; membership need not sum to 1.
>
> ✅ **Fuzzy Operations:** Union = MAX, Intersection = MIN, Complement = $1 - \mu$.
>
> ✅ **Mamdani FIS Stages:** Fuzzification → Rule Evaluation → Aggregation → Defuzzification.
>
> ✅ **Defuzzification Methods:** Centroid (most common), Bisector, MOM, SOM, LOM.
>
> ✅ **Probability ≠ Possibility** — Probability handles randomness; Fuzzy handles linguistic imprecision.
>
> ✅ **Lotfi Zadeh** introduced Fuzzy Set Theory in **1965** — remember this year for viva questions.
>
> ✅ **Mamdani (1975)** proposed the first fuzzy controller for a steam engine.
>
> ✅ **Application Areas:** BN — medical diagnosis, spam filtering, fault detection. Fuzzy — AC, washing machine, ABS, camera AF, traffic control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Uncertainty Reasoning (e.g., Medical Diagnosis, Risk Assessment)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT521 - KNOWLEDGE ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications of Uncertainty Reasoning (e.g., Medical Diagnosis, Risk Assessment)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Uncertainty Reasoning** is the sub-field of Knowledge Engineering that deals with representing, propagating, and updating **imprecise, incomplete, or probabilistic knowledge** within an intelligent system so that the system can still draw **defensible conclusions** even when the input evidence is noisy, vague, or conflicting.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme PEAIT521 syllabus**, this topic is treated as the *practical bridge* between Module 3 (Reasoning under Uncertainty — Bayesian, CF, Dempster-Shafer, Fuzzy) and Module 5 (Expert Systems & Planning). The two canonical applications examined are:

1. **Medical Diagnosis** — *“Given the symptoms a patient reports, which disease is most likely?”*
2. **Risk Assessment** — *“Given a set of environmental, financial, or operational factors, what is the probability of a system failure or hazardous event?”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weather Forecaster"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a weather forecaster in Kerala during monsoon. You cannot be **100% sure** it will rain tomorrow even if the sky looks grey. Instead, you say: *"There is an 80% chance of rain."* That single number encodes **uncertainty**. A medical doctor does exactly the same thing: *"There is a 70% chance this fever is typhoid."* **Uncertainty Reasoning** is the formal mathematics that lets a machine express, combine, and update such "soft" numbers just like a human expert does.

### 1.3 The Four Canonical Computational Frameworks

| Framework | Type of Uncertainty Handled | KTU 2024 Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Bayesian (Probabilistic) Reasoning** | Randomness, statistical frequency | **High-Weight** |
| **Certainty Factors (MYCIN-style)** | Rule-based expert confidence | **High-Weight** |
| **Dempster-Shafer Belief Theory** | Ignorance + Evidential overlap | **Moderate-Weight** |
| **Fuzzy Logic** | Linguistic vagueness (e.g., "high fever") | **Moderate-Weight** |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Recalled

- **Probability mass $P(A)$** always lies in the closed interval $[0, 1]$.
- **Certainty Factor $CF$** lies in the interval $[-1, +1]$.
- **Belief ($\text{Bel}$) and Plausibility ($\text{Pl}$)** satisfy $\text{Bel}(A) \le P(A) \le \text{Pl}(A)$.
- **Fuzzy membership $\mu_A(x)$** lies in the continuous interval $[0, 1]$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Each Framework Exists — The "Why"

- **Bayesian Reasoning** is preferred when **reliable historical frequency data** is available (e.g., disease prevalence in a population).
- **Certainty Factors** are used when experts express **disjoint confidence** in a rule (e.g., *"If meningitis, then stiff neck — CF = 0.75"*) and historical probabilities are not known.
- **Dempster-Shafer** is chosen when we must distinguish **"no evidence"** from **"evidence for negation"** — crucial in risk assessment where sensor readings are sparse.
- **Fuzzy Logic** is essential for **linguistic variables** like *"high blood pressure"*, *"moderate risk"*, *"young patient"*.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorise This Table)

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Domain / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Diagnostic inference |
| 2 | **Posterior Odds Form** | $\dfrac{P(H \mid E)}{P(\neg H \mid E)} = \dfrac{P(E \mid H)}{P(E \mid \neg H)} \cdot \dfrac{P(H)}{P(\neg H)}$ | Medical / Risk odds |
| 3 | **Certainty Factor Definition** | $CF(H, E) = MB(H, E) - MD(H, E)$ | MYCIN-style expert rules |
| 4 | **Measure of Belief** | $MB(H, E) = \dfrac{\max\bigl(P(H \mid E),\, P(H)\bigr) - P(H)}{1 - P(H)}$ | Belief update |
| 5 | **Measure of Disbelief** | $MD(H, E) = \dfrac{P(H) - \min\bigl(P(H \mid E),\, P(H)\bigr)}{P(H)}$ | Disbelief update |
| 6 | **CF Conjunction** | $CF(A \land B, E) = \min\bigl(CF(A,E),\, CF(B,E)\bigr)$ | Two symptoms together |
| 7 | **CF Disjunction** | $CF(A \lor B, E) = \max\bigl(CF(A,E),\, CF(B,E)\bigr)$ | Two alternative hypotheses |
| 8 | **CF Chained Rule** | $CF(H, E) = CF(H, E_1) \cdot CF(H, E_2)$ | Independent evidence |
| 9 | **Dempster Combination** | $m_{12}(A) = \dfrac{1}{1-K}\sum_{B \cap C = A} m_1(B)\,m_2(C)$ | Sensor fusion |
| 10 | **Conflict Mass $K$** | $K = \sum_{B \cap C = \emptyset} m_1(B)\,m_2(C)$ | Total conflicting evidence |
| 11 | **Belief from m.f.** | $\text{Bel}(A) = \sum_{B \subseteq A} m(B)$ | Lower probability bound |
| 12 | **Plausibility** | $\text{Pl}(A) = 1 - \text{Bel}(\neg A)$ | Upper probability bound |
| 13 | **Fuzzy AND (t-norm)** | $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min\bigl(\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(x)\bigr)$ | Rule antecedent |
| 14 | **Fuzzy OR (s-norm / t-conorm)** | $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max\bigl(\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(x)\bigr)$ | Rule alternative |
| 15 | **Fuzzy NOT** | $\mu_{\neg A}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$ | Complement |
| 16 | **Defuzzification (Centroid)** | $x^\star = \dfrac{\int x \cdot \mu(x)\,dx}{\int \mu(x)\,dx}$ | Crisp output from fuzzy set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The absolute value bars inside table rows (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) have been deliberately written as $\vert$ and $\bigl(\bigr)$ pairs to avoid breaking markdown table syntax during KTU PDF rendering. **Do not** write `|x|` inside any table cell.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS) such as MYCIN, INTERNIST-I, QMR use CF and Bayesian engines to assist physicians in ICU and pathology labs.
- **Risk Assessment:** Nuclear plant safety, aviation system reliability (FAA), flood forecasting (Kerala KSDMA), and financial credit scoring all use Bayesian + Dempster-Shafer hybrids.
- **Cybersecurity:** Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) use Bayesian networks to fuse noisy alerts from multiple sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Bayesian Medical Diagnosis

**Scenario:** In a Kerala coastal district, the prevalence of **Dengue** is $P(D) = 0.01$. The probability that a dengue patient has a **high platelet-drop** is $P(E \mid D) = 0.90$. The false positive rate (no dengue, but shows the symptom) is $P(E \mid \neg D) = 0.05$. A patient walks in showing the symptom. Find the **posterior probability** that he actually has dengue.

**Step 1 — State the given quantities:**

$$
P(D) = 0.01, \quad P(\neg D) = 0.99, \quad P(E \mid D) = 0.90, \quad P(E \mid \neg D) = 0.05
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total probability of evidence using the Law of Total Probability:**

$$
P(E) = P(E \mid D)\,P(D) + P(E \mid \neg D)\,P(\neg D)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
P(E) = (0.90)(0.01) + (0.05)(0.99)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each product:**

$$
P(E) = 0.0090 + 0.0495
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the products:**

$$
P(E) = 0.0585
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Bayes' Theorem to obtain the posterior:**

$$
P(D \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid D)\,P(D)}{P(E)}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute all numerical values into Bayes' formula:**

$$
P(D \mid E) = \frac{(0.90)(0.01)}{0.0585}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
(0.90)(0.01) = 0.009
$$

**Step 9 — Divide numerator by denominator:**

$$
P(D \mid E) = \frac{0.009}{0.0585} = 0.1538
$$

**Step 10 — Convert to a percentage and state the conclusion:**

$$
P(D \mid E) \approx 15.38\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** Even though the symptom is **90% reliable** when dengue is present, the *base rate* of dengue is so low that the patient is only **15.38% likely** to actually have it. This is the famous **"Base-Rate Fallacy"** that Bayesian reasoning corrects — a classic KTU exam trap.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Certainty Factor Combination (MYCIN-style)

**Scenario:** An expert system for cardiac risk has two independent rules:

- Rule 1: *"If chest pain $\to$ Heart Attack"* with $CF_1 = CF(H, E_1) = 0.7$
- Rule 2: *"If ECG abnormality $\to$ Heart Attack"* with $CF_2 = CF(H, E_2) = 0.8$

Both pieces of evidence are observed. Find the **combined Certainty Factor**.

**Step 1 — Recall the CF chaining formula for independent evidence:**

$$
CF_{combined} = CF_1 + CF_2 - (CF_1 \times CF_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the two certainty factors:**

$$
CF_{combined} = 0.7 + 0.8 - (0.7 \times 0.8)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product term:**

$$
0.7 \times 0.8 = 0.56
$$

**Step 4 — Add the two CFs and subtract the product:**

$$
CF_{combined} = 1.5 - 0.56
$$

**Step 5 — Final combined certainty:**

$$
CF_{combined} = 0.94
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Two moderately strong pieces of evidence (0.7 and 0.8) combine to yield a **very high** confidence of 0.94. The system will now strongly recommend a cardiac intervention.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Dempster-Shafer Sensor Fusion for Risk Assessment

**Scenario:** Two sensors $S_1$ and $S_2$ monitor a chemical plant. Their Basic Probability Assignments (BPAs) on the frame $\{\text{Safe}, \text{Leak}\}$ are:

- $m_1(\text{Safe}) = 0.6$, $m_1(\text{Leak}) = 0.3$, $m_1(\Theta) = 0.1$
- $m_2(\text{Safe}) = 0.5$, $m_2(\text{Leak}) = 0.4$, $m_2(\Theta) = 0.1$

**Step 1 — Compute the conflict mass $K$ (sum over all empty intersections):**

$$
K = m_1(\text{Safe})\,m_2(\text{Leak}) + m_1(\text{Leak})\,m_2(\text{Safe})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
K = (0.6)(0.4) + (0.3)(0.5)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each product:**

$$
K = 0.24 + 0.15
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two conflict contributions:**

$$
K = 0.39
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the combined mass on $\{\text{Safe}\}$:**

$$
m_{12}(\text{Safe}) = \frac{m_1(\text{Safe})\,m_2(\text{Safe}) + m_1(\text{Safe})\,m_2(\Theta) + m_1(\Theta)\,m_2(\text{Safe})}{1 - K}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the numerical values into the numerator:**

$$
\text{Num}_{\text{Safe}} = (0.6)(0.5) + (0.6)(0.1) + (0.1)(0.5)
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate each product term in the numerator:**

$$
\text{Num}_{\text{Safe}} = 0.30 + 0.06 + 0.05
$$

**Step 8 — Sum the three terms in the numerator:**

$$
\text{Num}_{\text{Safe}} = 0.41
$$

**Step 9 — Apply the normalising denominator:**

$$
m_{12}(\text{Safe}) = \frac{0.41}{1 - 0.39} = \frac{0.41}{0.61}
$$

**Step 10 — Final combined belief that the plant is Safe:**

$$
m_{12}(\text{Safe}) \approx 0.6721
$$

**Step 11 — Symmetrically compute the combined mass on $\{\text{Leak}\}$:**

$$
m_{12}(\text{Leak}) = \frac{m_1(\text{Leak})\,m_2(\text{Leak}) + m_1(\text{Leak})\,m_2(\Theta) + m_1(\Theta)\,m_2(\text{Leak})}{1 - K}
$$

**Step 12 — Substitute numerical values into the numerator:**

$$
\text{Num}_{\text{Leak}} = (0.3)(0.4) + (0.3)(0.1) + (0.1)(0.4)
$$

**Step 13 — Evaluate each product in the numerator:**

$$
\text{Num}_{\text{Leak}} = 0.12 + 0.03 + 0.04
$$

**Step 14 — Sum the three terms:**

$$
\text{Num}_{\text{Leak}} = 0.19
$$

**Step 15 — Apply the normalising denominator:**

$$
m_{12}(\text{Leak}) = \frac{0.19}{0.61} \approx 0.3115
$$

**Step 16 — Verification — masses should sum to 1:**

$$
0.6721 + 0.3115 + m_{12}(\Theta) = 1
$$

$$
m_{12}(\Theta) \approx 0.0164
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** After fusing both sensors via Dempster-Shafer, the belief that the plant is **Safe** rises from individual 0.6 and 0.5 to a combined 0.6721, while the **Leak** hypothesis is downgraded to 0.3115. The operator can now make a confident control-room decision.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Fuzzy Logic for Medical "High Fever"

**Scenario:** A fuzzy system has two membership functions:

- "Temperature 38.5 °C" $\to \mu_{\text{Fever}} = 0.6$
- "Temperature 38.5 °C" $\to \mu_{\text{HighFever}} = 0.3$

A rule says: *"IF (Fever AND HighFever) THEN Likely Dengue"*. Use the **MIN t-norm** for AND.

**Step 1 — Apply the MIN t-norm for conjunction:**

$$
\mu_{\text{antecedent}} = \min(0.6, 0.3)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the minimum:**

$$
\mu_{\text{antecedent}} = 0.3
$$

**Step 3 — State the rule firing strength (used in defuzzification step):**

$$
\text{Firing Strength} = 0.3
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bayesian Network for Medical Diagnosis (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Patient Exposure] --> nodeB[Infection Present]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Fever Symptom]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Rash Symptom]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Positive Serology]
    nodeF[Age Factor] --> nodeB
    nodeG[Season Monsoon] --> nodeA
    nodeC --> nodeH[Diagnosis Dengue]
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Treatment Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above is a **conditional-dependence Bayesian network**. Each arrow is a **directed probabilistic dependency**. The joint probability factorises as $P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_i P\bigl(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i)\bigr)$.

### 4.2 Dempster-Shafer Fusion Block Diagram for Risk Assessment

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS1[Sensor 1 Evidence] --> nodeBPA1[BPA m1]
    nodeS2[Sensor 2 Evidence] --> nodeBPA2[BPA m2]
    nodeBPA1 --> nodeComb[Dempster Combination Rule]
    nodeBPA2 --> nodeComb
    nodeComb --> nodeNorm[Normalise by 1 minus K]
    nodeNorm --> nodeOut[Combined Belief Bel A and Pl A]
    nodeOut --> nodeDec[Risk Mitigation Decision]
```

### 4.3 Certainty-Factor Inference Engine (MYCIN-style Pipeline)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSym[Observed Symptoms] --> nodePat[Pattern Matcher]
    nodePat --> nodeR1[Rule 1 Antecedent Match]
    nodePat --> nodeR2[Rule 2 Antecedent Match]
    nodeR1 --> nodeCF1[CF equals 0.7]
    nodeR2 --> nodeCF2[CF equals 0.8]
    nodeCF1 --> nodeComb[CF Combination CF1 plus CF2 minus CF1 times CF2]
    nodeCF2 --> nodeComb
    nodeComb --> nodeRec[Clinical Recommendation]
```

### 4.4 Fuzzy Logic Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeIn[Crisp Input e.g. 38.5 C] --> nodeFuzz[Fuzzifier Membership Functions]
    nodeFuzz --> nodeRule[Rule Base If Fever Then Risk]
    nodeRule --> nodeEngine[Inference Engine Min Max]
    nodeEngine --> nodeAgg[Aggregation of Output Sets]
    nodeAgg --> nodeDefuzz[Defuzzifier Centroid]
    nodeDefuzz --> nodeOut[Crisp Risk Score]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Certainty Factor. How is it different from conditional probability?*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Certainty Factor (CF)** is a numerical measure of an expert's confidence in a hypothesis $H$ given evidence $E$, defined formally as $CF(H, E) = MB(H, E) - MD(H, E)$, where $MB$ is the measure of belief and $MD$ is the measure of disbelief. The CF lies in the closed interval $[-1, +1]$, where $+1$ indicates absolute certainty, $-1$ indicates absolute disbelief, and $0$ indicates total ignorance. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for CF = MB - MD formula, 1 mark for difference from probability: CF allows negative values expressing disbelief, while $P(H \mid E)$ is always non-negative and constrained by the laws of probability; CFs do not require a complete probabilistic model).**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three differences between Bayesian reasoning and Dempster-Shafer theory.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per difference):**
> 1. **Bayesian** requires a *prior* probability $P(H)$ for every hypothesis, whereas **Dempster-Shafer** permits assigning belief mass to the entire frame $\Theta$, thereby representing *ignorance* explicitly.
> 2. **Bayesian** belief satisfies $P(H) + P(\neg H) = 1$ exactly, whereas in **Dempster-Shafer** we only have the bounds $\text{Bel}(A) \le P(A) \le \text{Pl}(A)$.
> 3. **Bayesian** demands conditional independence of evidence, whereas **Dempster-Shafer** can combine *correlated* evidence through its normalisation factor $(1-K)$.

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bayesian approach to medical diagnosis** with a suitable block diagram. Derive **Bayes' Theorem** from the product rule of probability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition and Context (2 marks):** In medical diagnosis, we wish to find the most probable disease $H_i$ from a set $\{H_1, H_2, \ldots, H_n\}$ given a set of observed symptoms $E$. Bayesian reasoning uses *prior* knowledge of disease prevalence and *likelihoods* of symptoms to compute the *posterior* probability $P(H_i \mid E)$.

2. **Derivation of Bayes' Theorem (3 marks):** Starting from the product rule:
$$
P(H \cap E) = P(H \mid E) \cdot P(E) = P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)
$$
Equating and dividing by $P(E)$:
$$
P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}
$$
For a set of mutually exclusive diseases $\{H_1, \ldots, H_n\}$, the denominator is expanded using the law of total probability:
$$
P(E) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)
$$
Hence the general form used in clinical decision support:
$$
P(H_i \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H_i) \cdot P(H_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(E \mid H_j) \cdot P(H_j)}
$$

3. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Diagram should show the flow: *Prior* $\to$ *Likelihood* $\to$ *Posterior*, with the **Symptom Evidence $E$** as the conditioning input feeding into the inference engine, producing a ranked list of diseases.

**(b)** Consider a diagnostic scenario with two diseases $D_1$ and $D_2$ where $P(D_1) = 0.4$, $P(D_2) = 0.6$, $P(E \mid D_1) = 0.75$, and $P(E \mid D_2) = 0.30$. Find the most likely disease given the evidence $E$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $P(E)$ using the law of total probability:**

$$
P(E) = P(E \mid D_1)\,P(D_1) + P(E \mid D_2)\,P(D_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
P(E) = (0.75)(0.4) + (0.30)(0.6)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each product:**

$$
P(E) = 0.30 + 0.18
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the products:**

$$
P(E) = 0.48
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the posterior for $D_1$ using Bayes' theorem:**

$$
P(D_1 \mid E) = \frac{(0.75)(0.4)}{0.48}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
(0.75)(0.4) = 0.30
$$

**Step 7 — Divide by the denominator:**

$$
P(D_1 \mid E) = \frac{0.30}{0.48} = 0.625
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the posterior for $D_2$:**

$$
P(D_2 \mid E) = \frac{(0.30)(0.6)}{0.48}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
(0.30)(0.6) = 0.18
$$

**Step 10 — Divide by the denominator:**

$$
P(D_2 \mid E) = \frac{0.18}{0.48} = 0.375
$$

**Step 11 — Compare the two posteriors and conclude:**

$$
P(D_1 \mid E) = 0.625 > P(D_2 \mid E) = 0.375
$$

**Step 12 — State the diagnostic conclusion:**

> The most likely disease is **$D_1$** with a posterior probability of **62.5%**.

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **Dempster-Shafer theory of evidence** with its key concepts of *frame of discernment*, *basic probability assignment (BPA)*, *belief function* $\text{Bel}(A)$, and *plausibility function* $\text{Pl}(A)$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Frame of Discernment $\Theta$ (1 mark):** The set of all mutually exclusive and exhaustive hypotheses. For a medical diagnosis, $\Theta = \{\text{Dengue}, \text{Typhoid}, \text{Malaria}\}$.

2. **Basic Probability Assignment $m$ (2 marks):** A function $m: 2^\Theta \to [0,1]$ such that $m(\emptyset) = 0$ and $\sum_{A \subseteq \Theta} m(A) = 1$. Unlike Bayesian probability, $m$ can be assigned to *subsets* of $\Theta$, not just singletons. The mass on the full set $\Theta$ represents *ignorance*.

3. **Belief Function $\text{Bel}(A)$ (2 marks):** The total mass committed to subsets *of* $A$:
$$
\text{Bel}(A) = \sum_{B \subseteq A} m(B)
$$
This is the *lower* probability bound, i.e., the minimum support we are willing to commit.

4. **Plausibility Function $\text{Pl}(A)$ (1 mark):** The maximum support that could be assigned to $A$ given the evidence:
$$
\text{Pl}(A) = 1 - \text{Bel}(\neg A)
$$
The interval $[\text{Bel}(A), \text{Pl}(A)]$ is the **evidential interval** representing the *epistemic uncertainty* about $A$.

5. **Key advantage over Bayesian (1 mark):** D-S theory lets us represent **"I do not know"** by assigning mass to $\Theta$ itself, whereas Bayesian methods force a precise prior even when we are ignorant.

**(b)** Two independent experts provide BPAs for a flood risk in a Kerala district. Their BPAs on the frame $\Theta = \{\text{Safe}, \text{Risk}\}$ are:

- Expert 1: $m_1(\text{Safe}) = 0.5$, $m_1(\text{Risk}) = 0.3$, $m_1(\Theta) = 0.2$
- Expert 2: $m_2(\text{Safe}) = 0.4$, $m_2(\text{Risk}) = 0.5$, $m_2(\Theta) = 0.1$

Apply **Dempster's rule of combination** to find the fused mass on $\{\text{Risk}\}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the conflict mass $K$:**

$$
K = m_1(\text{Safe})\,m_2(\text{Risk}) + m_1(\text{Risk})\,m_2(\text{Safe})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
K = (0.5)(0.5) + (0.3)(0.4)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each product:**

$$
K = 0.25 + 0.12
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the conflict contributions:**

$$
K = 0.37
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the combined mass on $\{\text{Risk}\}$ via Dempster's rule:**

$$
m_{12}(\text{Risk}) = \frac{m_1(\text{Risk})\,m_2(\text{Risk}) + m_1(\text{Risk})\,m_2(\Theta) + m_1(\Theta)\,m_2(\text{Risk})}{1 - K}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute numerical values into the numerator:**

$$
\text{Num} = (0.3)(0.5) + (0.3)(0.1) + (0.2)(0.5)
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate each product term:**

$$
\text{Num} = 0.15 + 0.03 + 0.10
$$

**Step 8 — Sum the three terms in the numerator:**

$$
\text{Num} = 0.28
$$

**Step 9 — Apply the normalising denominator $1 - K$:**

$$
m_{12}(\text{Risk}) = \frac{0.28}{1 - 0.37} = \frac{0.28}{0.63}
$$

**Step 10 — Final combined mass on the Risk hypothesis:**

$$
m_{12}(\text{Risk}) \approx 0.4444
$$

**Step 11 — State the engineering interpretation:**

> After fusing the two expert opinions, the *belief* that the region is at risk is **0.4444**, which is *higher* than either individual expert's 0.3 or 0.5 (when normalised). The emergency management authority should activate **flood-level-2** preparedness protocols.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing $P(H \mid E)$ with $P(E \mid H)$.** This is the single most common error. The posterior is $P(H \mid E)$, not the likelihood. **Marks lost: 2 to 3 out of 7.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Law of Total Probability in the denominator.** Students often write $P(H \mid E) = P(E \mid H)\,P(H)$ only, omitting the denominator. **Marks lost: 2 out of 7.**
> 3. **In Dempster-Shafer, omitting the normalising factor $(1-K)$.** Even if the numerator is correct, omitting the normalisation will lose **1 full mark**.
> 4. **In CF combination, using simple addition instead of $CF_1 + CF_2 - CF_1 \cdot CF_2$.** **Marks lost: 2 out of 7.**
> 5. **Writing $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table cell** — this breaks KTU's PDF rendering pipeline. Use `\vert x\vert` or `|x|` *outside* tables only.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorise These Before KTU Exam Day:**
> - **Bayes' Theorem** is the *master formula*; remember both the **conditional form** and the **odds form**.
> - **Base-Rate Fallacy:** Even a 99% accurate test gives low posterior probability when the disease is rare. Always show the denominator calculation.
> - **Certainty Factor $CF \in [-1, +1]$** is signed; positive means belief, negative means disbelief, zero means ignorance.
> - **CF combination formula:** $CF_{12} = CF_1 + CF_2 - CF_1 \cdot CF_2$ for *parallel independent evidence*.
> - **CF chaining:** $CF(H, E) = CF(H, E_1) \cdot CF(H, E_2)$ for *sequential evidence*.
> - **CF conjunction** uses $\min$, **disjunction** uses $\max$.
> - **Dempster-Shafer BPA** can be assigned to *subsets* of the frame, not only singletons — this is its key advantage over Bayes.
> - **Belief interval:** $[\text{Bel}(A), \text{Pl}(A)]$ always contains the *true* probability.
> - **Conflict mass $K$** must be subtracted via the $1/(1-K)$ normalisation.
> - **Fuzzy Logic** handles linguistic terms like *"high fever"*, *"moderate risk"* via membership $\mu \in [0,1]$.
> - **Defuzzification (Centroid)** converts the aggregated fuzzy output into a crisp engineering decision.
> - **Application Domains to Recall:** MYCIN (medical CF), QMR-DT (Bayesian medical), Pathfinder (lymph-node pathology), DST for sensor fusion in flood / nuclear / aviation risk.
> - **Always** state the *engineering interpretation* of any numerical result — KTU awards 1 mark for the final conclusion sentence.
